name: Cherokee Purple
description: Famously rich flavor and texture make this a colorful favorite among heirloom enthusiasts. Medium-large, flattened globe, 8-12 oz. fruits. Color is dusky pink with dark shoulders. Multilocular interior ranges from purple to brown to green. Relatively short vines. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil is at least 60°F.
flavor_profile: Rich, sweet, and smoky with complex earthy undertones
culinary_uses: Fresh slicing, sandwiches, salads, BLTs. Not ideal for canning due to low acidity.
disease_resistance: Limited disease resistance typical of heirlooms. Susceptible to late blight and cracking.
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, whiteflies, flea beetles
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, fusarium wilt, blossom end rot

name: Better Boy
description: One of the most popular hybrid tomatoes for good reason - this reliable performer produces heavy yields of large, flavorful fruits with excellent disease resistance. Perfect for beginner gardeners who want guaranteed success and experienced growers who appreciate consistent performance. The classic red slicing tomato that delivers on both quantity and quality.
soil_type: Well-drained garden soil with moderate organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant outdoors when soil temperature reaches 60°F.
flavor_profile: Balanced sweet-tart flavor with classic tomato taste
culinary_uses: Versatile for slicing, sandwiches, salads, cooking, and canning
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to Verticillium wilt, Fusarium wilt, and Nematodes (VFN)
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, cutworms, aphids
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, bacterial spot

name: San Marzano
description: San Marzano tomatoes are a heritage Italian plum variety prized for sauce production. These elongated, deep red fruits mature in approximately 60 days and feature distinctly few seeds and thin walls—ideal characteristics for canning and paste-making. Known for their sweet flavor profile with notably low acidity and intense, concentrated tomato taste, San Marzanos are the traditional choice for authentic Italian sauces. While classified as hybrid here, true San Marzano varieties are often open-pollinated heirlooms. The plants require full sun and rich, well-drained soil, with moderate to difficult cultivation demands.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter content
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Requires warm soil (65°F+) for transplanting.
flavor_profile: Sweet with low acidity, intense tomato flavor, minimal seeds
culinary_uses: Premium sauce making, paste, canning, pizza sauce, marinara
disease_resistance: Early Blight (Intermediate); Late Blight (Intermediate)
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, spider mites, whiteflies
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, fusarium wilt, bacterial canker

name: Celebrity
description: Celebrity is a classic hybrid tomato that matures in 60 days, producing large, deep red, globe-shaped fruits ideal for slicing and general-purpose use. Known for its dependable yields and disease resistance, Celebrity has been a home gardener favorite for decades. The fruit offers a well-balanced, classic tomato flavor with pleasant acidity, making it excellent for fresh eating, salads, and cooking. Its vigorous plant habit and consistent performance in full sun conditions make it a reliable choice for both beginners and experienced growers seeking tried-and-true productivity.
soil_type: Well-drained garden soil, adapts to various soil types
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant after soil warms to 60°F.
flavor_profile: Well-balanced, classic tomato flavor with good acidity
culinary_uses: Excellent for slicing, salads, sandwiches, and general cooking
disease_resistance: Early Blight (Intermediate); Late Blight (Intermediate)
common_pests: Hornworms, cutworms, flea beetles
common_diseases: Late blight, bacterial spot, blossom end rot

name: Brandywine
description: We describe Brandywine's luscious, heirloom flavor as "very rich, loud, and distinctively spicy." The large fruits, often over 1 lb., have a deep pink skin and smooth red flesh. The medium-tall, potato-leaf plant is best staked or caged. Our 'Quisenberry' strain is considered among the best. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Rich, deep, well-drained soil with abundant organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Requires strong support system due to large fruit size.
color: Pink to rose-pink with some yellow shoulders
flavor_profile: Intensely sweet, rich, and complex with perfect acid balance
culinary_uses: Premium fresh eating, slicing for special occasions, not suitable for canning
disease_resistance: Very limited disease resistance, requires preventive care
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, spider mites, cutworms
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, fusarium wilt, cracking, blossom end rot

name: Roma
description: Roma is a classic heirloom paste tomato prized for its dense, meaty flesh and concentrated flavor. The plant produces elongated, plum-shaped fruits that mature in 60-65 days with minimal seeds and low acidity. Known for its mild, sweet taste, Roma excels in fresh sauces, pastes, and cooking applications where robust texture matters. This reliable, easy-to-grow variety tolerates less fertile soil and requires minimal maintenance. Its naturally rich flavor develops without excessive acidity, making it ideal for traditional Italian tomato preparations.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates less fertile conditions
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Direct sow in warm climates when soil reaches 65°F.
flavor_profile: Concentrated, less acidic, mild and sweet
culinary_uses: Ideal for sauces, paste, canning, drying, and cooking. Can be eaten fresh but bred for processing.
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to common tomato diseases. Less susceptible to cracking than slicing varieties.
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, whiteflies
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, fusarium wilt

name: Cherry Belle
description: A prolific cherry tomato that produces hundreds of sweet, bite-sized fruits throughout the growing season. These vigorous indeterminate plants are perfect for snacking straight from the garden and add color to salads and appetizers. Kids love picking and eating these candy-like tomatoes right off the vine.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Can direct sow in warm climates 2-3 weeks after last frost date.
flavor_profile: Very sweet with bright, intense tomato flavor and slight tartness
culinary_uses: Perfect for snacking, salads, garnishes, roasting whole, and preserving. Great for kids' gardens.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to cracking and splitting. Moderate disease resistance typical of cherry varieties.
common_pests: Birds, tomato hornworm, aphids, spider mites
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, powdery mildew

name: Early Girl
description: A longtime early favorite, Early Girl produces heavy yields of full-flavored, 4-6 oz. tomatoes. One of the first varieties to ripen each year. Also prized for its performance as a "dry-farmed" tomato, owing to its extreme tolerance to drought and blossom end rot. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant when soil reaches 60°F and night temperatures stay above 50°F.
flavor_profile: Well-balanced sweet-tart flavor with good acidity
culinary_uses: Excellent for slicing, sandwiches, salads, and general cooking. Good for canning and sauces.
disease_resistance: Fusarium Wilt races 1, 2 (High); Verticillium Wilt (High)
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, cutworms, spider mites
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, bacterial speck, blossom end rot

name: Big Beef
description: Still unsurpassed as the top choice for fresh market beefsteak tomatoes. Large, avg. 10-12 oz., mostly blemish-free, globe-shaped red fruit. They have full flavor - among the best - and ripen early for their size. Broad disease resistance package. AAS Winner. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with high organic content
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant outdoors when soil temperature reaches 65°F.
color: Deep red with slight pink undertones
flavor_profile: Rich, full-bodied tomato flavor with perfect sweet-acid balance
culinary_uses: Premium slicing tomato for sandwiches and fresh eating. Excellent for stuffing and grilling.
disease_resistance: Alternaria Stem Canker (High); Fusarium Wilt races 1, 2 (High); Gray Leaf Spot (High); Nematodes (High); Tobacco Mosaic Virus (High); Verticillium Wilt (High)
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, whiteflies, spider mites
common_diseases: Late blight, bacterial spot, septoria leaf spot

name: Green Zebra
description: A delicious, tangy salad tomato, ripe just as the green fruit develops a yellow blush, accentuating the darker green stripes. The 3-4 oz. fruits are the ideal size for slicing into wedges for salads. Productive over a long season. Developed by Tom Wagner. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with organic matter
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant when nighttime temperatures stay above 55°F.
color: Green with dark green stripes, yellow undertones when ripe
flavor_profile: Tangy, zesty, and tart with bright acidity and citrus notes
culinary_uses: Excellent for fresh eating, salads, salsas, and garnishes. Creates stunning fried green tomatoes.
disease_resistance: Moderate disease resistance, better than most heirlooms. Some resistance to cracking.
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, whiteflies, spider mites
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, fusarium wilt, bacterial speck

name: Beefsteak
description: The classic giant of the tomato world, producing massive fruits that can weigh over a pound each. These impressive slicing tomatoes have been a garden favorite for generations, offering substantial, meaty slices perfect for sandwiches and burgers. Their size and reliable production make them a must-have for gardeners who want to impress with truly spectacular harvests.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with high organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant when soil reaches 65°F and nighttime temps stay above 55°F.
shape: Large, round to slightly flattened, ribbed
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet, and balanced with low acidity
culinary_uses: Premium slicing for sandwiches, burgers, fresh eating. Excellent stuffed. Too large for most canning.
disease_resistance: Limited resistance; requires good garden hygiene and air circulation
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, spider mites, cutworms
common_diseases: Blossom end rot, cracking, late blight, fusarium wilt

name: Mortgage Lifter
description: This legendary heirloom tomato earned its name when creator M.C. Byles paid off his mortgage by selling seedlings for $1 each during the Depression. These massive pink beefsteak tomatoes can weigh up to 4 pounds and offer exceptional flavor with meaty texture and few seeds. Perfect for gardeners who want to grow truly impressive conversation-starter tomatoes.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with plenty of compost
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant when soil is consistently above 60°F.
shape: Large beefsteak, slightly flattened with moderate ribbing
flavor_profile: Sweet and rich with perfect balance, minimal acidity
culinary_uses: Premium fresh slicing, sandwiches, salads. Single slice can cover an entire sandwich. Not suitable for canning.
disease_resistance: Typical heirloom susceptibility; good air circulation essential
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, spider mites, whiteflies
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, blossom end rot, cracking in wet weather

name: Stupice
description: This Czechoslovakian heirloom is prized as one of the earliest full-flavored tomatoes, often ripening weeks before other varieties. Despite its early maturity, it delivers complex, rich flavor that rivals the best season tomatoes. Perfect for short-season gardeners who don't want to sacrifice taste for earliness.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, adaptable to various soil types
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Can tolerate cooler transplant conditions than most varieties.
flavor_profile: Rich, complex flavor with good balance of sweet and tart
culinary_uses: Excellent fresh eating, salads, sandwiches. Good for sauce and canning due to balanced flavor.
disease_resistance: Good cold tolerance, moderate disease resistance for an heirloom
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, tomato hornworm
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, but generally more resistant than many heirlooms

name: Black Krim
description: Black Krim combines bold, smoky flavor and good texture with an unusual appearance. Deep brown/red, 8-16 oz. tomatoes have brown/green shoulders that get darker with more heat and sunlight. High-yielding. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant after soil warms to 65°F.
flavor_profile: Rich, complex, and smoky with sweet undertones and mild saltiness
culinary_uses: Gourmet fresh slicing, specialty salads, farmers market sales. Beautiful contrast in dishes. Low acid - not ideal for canning.
disease_resistance: Typical heirloom susceptibility, prone to cracking in wet conditions
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, whiteflies, spider mites
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, blossom end rot, fruit cracking

name: Yellow Pear
description: A charming heirloom cherry tomato dating back to the 1800s, producing abundant clusters of bright yellow, pear-shaped fruits that are as decorative as they are delicious. This prolific variety is beloved by children and adults alike for its sweet, mild flavor and unique teardrop shape that adds visual interest to any garden or salad.
soil_type: Well-drained garden soil, adaptable
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after last frost date when soil is warm.
flavor_profile: Sweet and mild with low acidity, fruity undertones
culinary_uses: Fresh snacking, salads, garnishes, children's gardens. Beautiful in mixed dishes. Can be used for unique yellow preserves.
disease_resistance: Generally hardy with good disease tolerance for an heirloom
common_pests: Aphids, whiteflies, spider mites, birds (love the sweet fruit)
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, minimal cracking due to small size

name: Rutgers
description: A legendary heirloom variety developed at Rutgers University in 1934 that became the standard for tomato flavor in America. This reliable producer delivers perfectly balanced, old-fashioned tomato taste in medium-sized fruits that are ideal for both fresh eating and processing. Rutgers represents the classic American tomato that many gardeners remember from their childhood.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil is at least 60°F.
flavor_profile: Classic balanced tomato flavor with good acidity and sweetness
culinary_uses: Excellent for fresh eating, slicing, canning, sauces, and paste. Perfect all-purpose tomato.
disease_resistance: Good overall disease resistance for an heirloom variety
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, whiteflies, cutworms
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, fusarium wilt, bacterial spot

name: Patio Princess
description: Specially bred for container growing, Patio Princess produces full-sized tomatoes on compact, sturdy plants that thrive in pots and small spaces. Despite its diminutive size, this determinate variety delivers surprising yields of 4-6 oz red tomatoes with excellent flavor. Perfect for urban gardeners, balcony growers, and anyone wanting fresh tomatoes without a large garden space.
soil_type: Well-drained potting mix with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct seed when soil temperature reaches 65°F. Ideal for 5+ gallon containers.
flavor_profile: Sweet and tangy with good tomato flavor for a compact variety
culinary_uses: Fresh slicing, salads, sandwiches, snacking. Good for small-batch cooking.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to verticillium wilt and fusarium wilt (VF)
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, whiteflies, thrips
common_diseases: Early blight, blossom end rot, bacterial speck

name: Amish Paste
description: Large for a sauce tomato, Amish Paste's slightly irregular plum to strawberry-shaped fruits avg. 8-12 oz. with excellent flavor. These meaty tomatoes are good in salads and great for processing. A Slow Food USA Ark of Taste variety. Indeterminate.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil is at least 60°F.
flavor_profile: Rich, sweet, and intense with low acidity - excellent concentrated tomato flavor
culinary_uses: Outstanding for sauces, paste, canning, and cooking. Good fresh eating despite being a paste type.
disease_resistance: Limited disease resistance typical of heirlooms. Some tolerance to cracking.
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, flea beetles, stink bugs
common_diseases: Late blight, early blight, fusarium wilt, bacterial canker

name: Mountain Fresh Plus
description: Able to tolerate cool and wet conditions, this big red tomato produces attractive, 8-16 oz. slicers with good flavor. Developed by Dr. Randy Gardner at North Carolina State University. Vigorous plants provide plenty of leaf cover. Determinate.
soil_type: Well-drained loam with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date. Transplant after all danger of frost has passed and soil is at least 60°F.
flavor_profile: Excellent balance of sweetness and acidity with rich tomato flavor
culinary_uses: Premium fresh eating, slicing, salads. Good for light cooking and fresh salsas.
disease_resistance: Fusarium Wilt races 1, 2 (High); Nematodes (High); Verticillium Wilt (High)
common_pests: Tomato hornworm, aphids, spider mites, thrips
common_diseases: Bacterial speck, blossom end rot (with inconsistent watering)

name: Surefire Red
description: An All-America Selections winner bred specifically for cool-weather performance and short growing seasons, producing ripe tomatoes even when temperatures dip below ideal ranges. This compact hybrid sets fruit reliably in conditions that cause other varieties to drop blossoms, making it perfect for northern gardens and unpredictable climates. Surefire Red delivers dependable harvests when gardening success seems uncertain.
soil_type: Well-drained garden soil with moderate fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Can be planted earlier than most tomatoes. Good for succession planting.
flavor_profile: Good balanced flavor with pleasant sweetness and mild acidity
culinary_uses: Excellent for fresh eating, salads, sandwiches, and light cooking. Perfect for beginning gardeners.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to verticillium wilt, fusarium wilt, and tobacco mosaic virus (VFT)
common_pests: Aphids, cutworms, flea beetles, whiteflies
common_diseases: Early blight, late blight, bacterial spot

name: Bell Pepper 'California Wonder'
description: The gold standard for sweet bell peppers, California Wonder has been America's favorite home garden variety since the 1920s. These thick-walled, blocky peppers start green and can ripen to brilliant red, offering exceptional sweetness and crunch. Reliable, productive, and perfect for beginners, it's the pepper that built America's love affair with home-grown sweet peppers.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam with good organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant when soil temperature reaches 65°F and nighttime temperatures stay above 55°F.
shape: Blocky, square-shouldered bell shape with 3-4 lobes
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild, and crisp with no heat
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, stuffing, roasting, grilling, stir-fries, salads, and preserving
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to tobacco mosaic virus
common_pests: Aphids, pepper weevil, flea beetles, hornworms
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, anthracnose, blossom end rot, pepper mosaic virus

name: Jalapeño 'Early Jalapeño'
description: The quintessential hot pepper for American gardens, offering the perfect balance of heat and flavor that made jalapeños famous. This early variety produces abundant harvests of thick-walled, 3-inch peppers with that distinctive jalapeño taste and moderate heat level. Whether you're making fresh salsa, poppers, or want to smoke them into chipotles, this reliable variety delivers authentic jalapeño flavor.
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, allow slight drying between waterings
soil_type: Well-drained sandy loam with moderate fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost date. Transplant after soil reaches 65°F and night temperatures stay above 55°F.
shape: Conical, slightly curved with blunt tip
flavor_profile: Moderate heat (2,500-8,000 Scoville) with bright, grassy flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh salsas, pickling, stuffing for poppers, hot sauces, Mexican cuisine, grilling
disease_resistance: Good resistance to tobacco mosaic virus and bacterial spot
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, pepper weevil, thrips
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, phytophthora blight, anthracnose, pepper mottle virus

name: Habanero 'Orange Habanero'
description: The classic superhot pepper that brings serious heat along with an incredible fruity, citrusy flavor that's become legendary among chili enthusiasts. These wrinkled, lantern-shaped peppers pack intense heat but reward brave gardeners with complex flavor notes of tropical fruit and floral undertones. A small amount goes a very long way in hot sauces, salsas, and Caribbean-inspired dishes.
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture but well-drained
soil_type: Well-drained sandy loam, avoid overly rich soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost. Requires warm soil (70-80°F) for germination. Transplant when nights stay above 60°F.
flavor_profile: Extremely hot (100,000-350,000 Scoville) with fruity, citrusy, tropical notes
culinary_uses: Hot sauces, Caribbean cuisine, salsas (use sparingly), drying for powder
disease_resistance: Generally good disease resistance, susceptible to root rot in wet conditions
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, whiteflies, pepper weevil
common_diseases: Root rot, bacterial spot, anthracnose, pepper mottle virus

name: Banana Pepper 'Sweet Banana'
description: The perfect mild pepper for gardeners who love the flavor of peppers without any heat whatsoever. Sweet Banana peppers are incredibly productive, producing loads of pale yellow, curved pods that add beautiful color and sweet crunch to any dish. Easy to grow and harvest continuously throughout the season, they're ideal for pickling, fresh eating, and adding to pizza and sandwiches.
soil_type: Well-drained fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant when soil temperature reaches 65°F consistently.
shape: Long, curved, tapered with pointed tip
flavor_profile: Sweet and mild with no heat, crisp texture
culinary_uses: Pickling, fresh eating, pizza toppings, sandwiches, salads, stuffing
disease_resistance: Good resistance to common pepper diseases
common_pests: Aphids, flea beetles, pepper weevil, cutworms
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, blossom end rot, pepper mosaic virus, anthracnose

name: Cayenne 'Long Red Cayenne'
description: The classic hot pepper that's been setting kitchens on fire since the 1800s, Long Red Cayenne is the go-to variety for homemade hot sauce and dried pepper flakes. These slender, curved peppers pack substantial heat while remaining incredibly useful in the kitchen, drying beautifully and grinding into the perfect pizza-shaking spice. Extremely productive and reliable, it's an essential variety for any serious pepper grower.
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained sandy loam, tolerates poor soils
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant when soil is consistently 65°F or warmer.
shape: Long, slender, curved with pointed tip
flavor_profile: Hot (30,000-50,000 Scoville) with clean, sharp heat and earthy undertones
culinary_uses: Hot sauces, drying for flakes, Cajun cuisine, pickling, fresh cooking (use sparingly)
disease_resistance: Good general disease resistance, hardy variety
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, pepper weevil, hornworms
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, anthracnose, pepper mosaic virus, phytophthora blight

name: Poblano 'Ancho Poblano'
description: The backbone of Mexican cuisine, this mild to medium-heat pepper is perfect for stuffing, roasting, and making authentic chiles rellenos. When fresh, they're called poblanos; when dried, they become the coveted ancho chile that adds deep, smoky flavor to sauces and moles. Their large size and thick walls make them incredibly versatile for both fresh cooking and preservation.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant outdoors 2-3 weeks after last frost when soil temperature reaches 60°F.
color: Dark green when fresh, deep reddish-brown when dried
shape: Heart-shaped with broad shoulders tapering to a point
flavor_profile: Mild to medium heat (1,000-2,000 Scoville) with rich, earthy flavor and subtle sweetness
culinary_uses: Chiles rellenos, roasting, stuffing, salsas, moles, drying for ancho chiles
disease_resistance: Good resistance to tobacco mosaic virus, moderate bacterial spot resistance
common_pests: Aphids, pepper weevil, cutworms, spider mites
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, phytophthora blight, pepper mottle virus

name: Serrano 'Tampiqueno'
description: A fiery favorite that packs serious heat into a small package, delivering 2-5 times the punch of a jalapeño with incredible productivity throughout the growing season. These slender peppers are essential for authentic Mexican salsas and hot sauces, offering a clean, bright heat that doesn't overpower the fresh pepper flavor. One plant produces hundreds of peppers, making it a must-have for hot pepper enthusiasts.
soil_type: Well-drained, sandy loam with good fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant 2-3 weeks after last frost date when nighttime temperatures stay above 55°F.
shape: Small, slender, cylindrical with pointed tip
flavor_profile: Very hot (10,000-25,000 Scoville) with bright, clean heat and fresh pepper flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh salsas, hot sauces, pickling, Mexican cuisine, drying
disease_resistance: Good overall disease resistance, tolerant of bacterial spot
common_pests: Aphids, thrips, pepper maggot, spider mites
common_diseases: Bacterial leaf spot, anthracnose, cucumber mosaic virus

name: Sweet Pepper 'Cubanelle'
description: Often called the 'Italian Frying Pepper,' this sweet, thin-walled variety is prized for its exceptional flavor and tender texture that makes it perfect for quick cooking. Popular in Caribbean and Italian cuisine, Cubanelle peppers offer a mild, sweet taste with just a hint of heat that appeals to all palates. Their elongated shape and vibrant color make them as beautiful in the garden as they are delicious on the plate.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant outdoors after soil warms to 60°F and night temperatures stay above 50°F.
color: Light green to yellow-green, ripening to red
shape: Long, tapered, slightly curved with thin walls
flavor_profile: Very mild and sweet with almost no heat (0-1,000 Scoville), crisp when fresh
culinary_uses: Frying, sautéing, stuffing, salads, sandwiches, Italian and Caribbean dishes
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to common pepper diseases
common_pests: Aphids, pepper weevil, European corn borer, flea beetles
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, phytophthora blight, pepper mottle virus

name: Thai Hot 'Thai Dragon'
description: This compact powerhouse delivers authentic Thai cuisine heat in an ornamental package that's as beautiful as it is blazing hot. The small, upward-pointing peppers start green and ripen through yellow, orange, and finally bright red, creating a stunning multicolored display while packing intense heat that's essential for pad thai, curries, and Asian stir-fries. Perfect for containers and small spaces, one plant produces hundreds of fiery little peppers.
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, allow slight drying between waterings
soil_type: Well-drained, moderately fertile soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant outdoors when nighttime temperatures consistently stay above 55°F.
color: Green ripening through yellow and orange to bright red
flavor_profile: Very hot (50,000-100,000 Scoville) with intense, clean heat and bright pepper flavor
culinary_uses: Thai cuisine, Asian stir-fries, hot sauces, drying, pickling
disease_resistance: Excellent disease resistance, tolerant of bacterial spot and pepper mottle virus
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, whiteflies
common_diseases: Generally disease resistant, occasional bacterial leaf spot

name: Shishito 'Japanese Shishito'
description: The darling of modern cuisine and gastropubs, these slender Japanese peppers offer addictive mild flavor with the thrilling surprise that about 1 in 10 packs a spicy punch. Perfect for blistering in a hot pan with just salt and olive oil, shishitos have become the go-to appetizer pepper for home cooks wanting to recreate restaurant favorites. Their prolific production and easy growing habit make them ideal for gardeners seeking something trendy yet reliable.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant outdoors 2-3 weeks after last frost when soil temperature reaches 60°F.
color: Bright green, can ripen to red if left on plant
shape: Slender, wrinkled, slightly curved with pointed tip
flavor_profile: Mostly mild and sweet (50-200 Scoville) with occasional hot pepper surprise
culinary_uses: Blistering in hot pan, grilling, tempura, Japanese cuisine, appetizers
disease_resistance: Good resistance to common pepper diseases
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, pepper weevil
common_diseases: Bacterial spot, anthracnose, pepper mottle virus

name: Anaheim 'New Mexico Big Jim'
description: The king of green chiles and a cornerstone of Southwestern cuisine, producing enormous 8-12 inch peppers with thick walls perfect for roasting and stuffing. These mild to medium-heat peppers are essential for authentic New Mexican dishes like green chile stew and chiles rellenos. The large size and meaty texture make them incredibly versatile, while the distinctive earthy-sweet flavor defines the taste of the Southwest.
soil_type: Well-drained sandy loam

description: Growers in the Pacific Northwest and Northern Tier of the US rely on Hungarian Hot Wax because it matures very early in cool, wet weather. However, many commercial strains have been poorly maintained; plants grown from these lots are often floppy, and the fruits vary in size, shape, and heat level. In 2015, our breeding team began improving this variety through 3 cycles of selective plant breeding. Our newly selected strain features an upright plant and uniform fruits. The greatly improved plant architecture offers better fruit protection and faster/easier harvests with minimal fruit damage! Smooth, tapered, and moderately spicy fruits avg. 5 1/2" x 1 1/2" and mature from pale yellow to orange red, and finally, to a vibrant red. Excellent for frying, roasting, or pickling.

description: This versatile, vigorous, mildew-resistant variety is excellent at a time of year when greens become scarce. Long, oval, glossy green leaves form tight rosettes. Mild, slightly nutty flavor also reminiscent of roses. Also known as mâche, lamb's lettuce, and corn salad. Unsized seed.
sun_requirement: Partial shade to full sun (3-6 hours)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, consistent light moisture
soil_type: Well-drained, moderately fertile soil
sowing_method: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in late summer or early fall. Seeds need cool soil to germinate properly.
flavor_profile: Mild, nutty, and slightly sweet with tender texture
culinary_uses: Gourmet salads, garnish, winter greens. Traditionally served with walnut oil and vinegar in French cuisine.
disease_resistance: Excellent cold tolerance and disease resistance. Very hardy in winter conditions.
common_pests: Slugs, aphids (rarely problematic)
common_diseases: Downy mildew in very wet conditions, otherwise disease-free

name: Genovese Basil
description: Authentic flavor and appearance. Tall and relatively slow to bolt with large dark green leaves about 3" long. This common strain of Genovese basil can present some variability in leaf shape and plant size. While quite suitable for field and garden, we recommend using a more modern and more uniform variety for greenhouse, container, or hydroponic production, such as Genovese Compact Improved, Aroma 2, Nufar, Dolly, or the Prospera® series. Ht. 24-30".Edible Flowers: Use the flowers in any recipe that calls for basil, or to garnish drinks, salads, soups, pasta, and desserts. Flavor is of intense basil.Also available in organic raw and pelleted seed.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

flavor_profile: Sweet, aromatic, and slightly spicy with hints of anise and clove

name: English Lavender
description: A compact, hardy lavender variety that's perfect for beginners and produces fragrant purple flower spikes beloved by bees and butterflies. This drought-tolerant perennial offers the classic lavender fragrance that's perfect for sachets, cooking, and aromatherapy while forming neat, silvery-green mounds in the garden. Munstead is one of the most cold-hardy lavender varieties, making it suitable for northern climates.

water_requirement: Low water needs once established, drought tolerant
soil_type: Well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil, poor to moderately fertile
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost. Seeds need cold stratification. Easier to start from cuttings or plants.
color: Purple flower spikes with silvery-green foliage
shape: Compact rounded bush with narrow linear leaves and cylindrical flower spikes
flavor_profile: Floral, sweet, and slightly camphor-like with classic lavender aroma

common_pests: Spittlebug, aphids, whiteflies (rarely problematic)

name: Flat-Leaf Parsley
description: Also known as Italian parsley, this variety offers superior flavor compared to curly parsley with its clean, bright taste that doesn't overpower dishes. The flat, deeply-cut leaves are easier to chop and provide a fresh, grassy flavor that's essential in Mediterranean cooking. This hardy biennial is incredibly productive and continues growing through light frosts, making it a kitchen garden staple.

growing_season: Cool season, biennial grown as annual
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring or start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Soak seeds 24 hours before planting to improve germination.

common_diseases: Crown rot, leaf spot, powdery mildew in humid conditions

name: Greek Oregano
description: Strong oregano aroma and flavor - great for pizza and Italian cooking. Characteristic dark green leaves with white flowers. Attracts Beneficial Insects: provides pollen and nectar for beneficial insects such as bees, hoverflies, lacewing larva, parasitic wasps, and tachinid flies. Edible Flowers: The flowers have an oregano flavor and can be used in the same manner as the herb. They are especially good in pasta salads, green salads, and as a fresh pizza topping.
water_requirement: Low to moderate water, drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in late spring. Can also propagate from cuttings or division.

flavor_profile: Intensely aromatic, pungent, and slightly bitter with warm, pine-like notes

disease_resistance: Very disease resistant, especially in well-drained soils
common_pests: Spider mites, aphids (rarely problematic)
common_diseases: Root rot in waterlogged soils, generally very healthy

name: Common Thyme
description: Common Thyme is a compact, woody perennial herb with tiny, narrow green leaves and delicate pink or purple flowers. Reaching maturity in 90-95 days, it becomes a robust ground cover perfect for edging and containers. This Mediterranean native thrives in poor, well-drained soil and is remarkably cold-hardy. Its earthy, minty flavor with warm, pungent undertones makes it essential for cooking—particularly valued in French cuisine and Mediterranean dishes. More cold-tolerant than most culinary herbs, Common Thyme delivers consistent flavor year-round and rarely suffers from serious pest issues.
water_requirement: Low water needs, very drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained, sandy or rocky soil, lean to moderately fertile
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Seeds are tiny and slow to germinate. Easier to propagate from cuttings or plant divisions.
color: Gray-green leaves with pink or white flowers
shape: Low, woody shrub with small linear leaves and clusters of tiny tubular flowers
flavor_profile: Earthy, minty, and slightly floral with warm, pungent undertones

common_diseases: Root rot in poorly drained soils, botrytis in humid conditions

name: Sweet Basil
description: Sweet Basil is a tender annual herb prized for its vibrant green leaves and distinctive aromatic flavor. This heirloom variety reaches harvest maturity in approximately 74 days, producing compact, bushy plants ideal for containers or garden beds. Its sweet, clove-forward taste with subtle anise undertones makes it the quintessential choice for Italian cuisine, fresh pestos, and culinary applications. Unlike spicier basil varieties, Sweet Basil's delicate, complex flavor profile and ease of cultivation have made it a kitchen garden staple for centuries.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil rich in organic matter

name: Curly Parsley
description: The finely curled dark blue-green leaves can be harvested with one cut. The compact plants produce leaves that are very uniform, resist yellowing, and have good flavor. Short internodes allow for high yields despite compact habit. Perfect for harvesting the whole plant at 12-18".

name: Garden Sage
description: High essential oil content of 1.5-2.0%. Robust, uniform plant. Also known as garden sage. Edible flowers: Use as garnish, or as an ingredient in rice, egg, or cheese dishes.

name: Cilantro
description: Cilantro is a delicate leafy herb with feathery, triangular green foliage that reaches harvest maturity in 16-25 days. The plant produces thin, lacy stems bearing distinctive serrated leaves with a bright, citrusy flavor profile that carries subtle soapy notes and fresh herbal undertones. Cilantro thrives in full sun to partial shade and prefers well-drained, fertile soil. As a quick-maturing heirloom variety, it's ideal for succession planting throughout the growing season. Widely used in Latin American, Asian, and Mediterranean cuisines as a fresh garnish and flavor component in salsas, curries, and chutneys.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring and fall, succession plant every 2-3 weeks

name: Gourmet Gold Dill
description: A stunning ornamental dill variety with bright golden-yellow foliage that adds both culinary value and visual drama to herb gardens. The vibrant color intensifies in cool weather while maintaining the classic dill flavor profile. Perfect for gardeners who want their herb garden to double as landscape decoration.

name: Pesto Perpetuo Basil
description: A spectacular variegated basil that never flowers, providing continuous harvest of cream and green striped leaves throughout the season. This sterile hybrid focuses all its energy on leaf production, making it the ultimate pesto-making machine. The striking variegation makes it as ornamental as it is functional.

name: Chocolate Mint
description: Chocolate Mint is a distinctive heirloom herb variety that matures in 65-75 days, featuring aromatic green foliage with soft, velvety texture. This unique mint hybrid stands out for its exceptional flavor profile: a refreshing cool mint taste with prominent chocolate undertones and a subtle sweet finish, making it ideal for both culinary applications and specialty beverages. Despite its rich flavor complexity, Chocolate Mint remains easy to grow, thriving in full sun with well-drained, moist soil. The plant tolerates heavy clay conditions well, making it adaptable to various garden settings.

name: Purple Ruffles Basil
description: An award-winning ornamental basil that combines stunning deep purple, frilly leaves with the classic sweet basil flavor gardeners love. This All-America Selections winner creates dramatic visual impact in both herb gardens and flower beds while providing excellent culinary value. Purple Ruffles transforms ordinary dishes into restaurant-quality presentations with its striking color and exceptional taste.

name: Italian Large Leaf Basil
description: Italian Large Leaf Basil is a classic heirloom herb prized for its broad, tender leaves and intensely aromatic, sweet basil flavor with subtle hints of anise and pepper. Reaching maturity in approximately 74 days, this variety produces robust plants suited for full sun to partial shade conditions. The large leaves make it exceptionally practical for culinary applications—ideal for fresh use in pesto, Italian dishes, and salads. Unlike compact basil varieties, its generous leaf size reduces preparation time while delivering the authentic, complex flavor profile that defines traditional Italian cooking.

name: French Tarragon
description: The aristocrat of culinary herbs, true French tarragon is essential for classic French cuisine with its distinctive anise-like flavor and sophisticated aroma. Unlike Russian tarragon, this sterile variety must be grown from cuttings and offers incomparably superior flavor. A must-have for serious cooks who want to create authentic béarnaise sauce and fine herb blends.

flavor_profile: Delicate anise flavor with hints of vanilla and pepper, slightly bitter finish
culinary_uses: Béarnaise sauce, fines herbes, chicken dishes, fish, salad dressings, vinegar infusions, egg dishes
disease_resistance: Generally disease resistant when grown in well-drained soil

common_diseases: Root rot, powdery mildew in humid conditions

name: Spicy Globe Basil
description: A charming miniature basil that forms perfect little mounds of tiny, intensely flavored leaves - ideal for container gardens and small spaces. Despite its diminutive size, this variety packs a powerful basil punch and naturally stays compact without constant pruning. The globe shape and small leaves make it as ornamental as it is useful, perfect for edging herb gardens or windowsill growing.

name: Silver Thyme
description: Silver Thyme is a classic heirloom herb prized for its distinctive silvery-green foliage and aromatic intensity. This easy-to-grow perennial reaches maturity in 120-150 days, producing delicate flowers that attract pollinators. The leaves deliver authentic thyme flavor—earthy, minty, and subtly floral—making it ideal for culinary applications, particularly Mediterranean dishes and herbal infusions. Hardy and drought-tolerant, Silver Thyme thrives in well-drained, sandy, or rocky soil with minimal care, distinguishing it through superior flavor complexity compared to common thyme varieties.

soil_type: Well-drained, sandy or rocky soil, tolerates poor soil

common_diseases: Root rot in poorly drained soil, generally very disease resistant

name: Lemon Balm
description: Lemon Balm is a vigorous perennial herb with soft, crinkled green leaves and small white or pale pink flowers. Reaching full maturity in 70 days, this heirloom variety thrives in full sun to partial shade and adapts well to most soil types. Distinguished by its intensely aromatic foliage releasing a powerful lemon scent when brushed, Lemon Balm delivers a refreshing citrus flavor with subtle minty undertones. Its strong essential oils naturally repel common pests like aphids and spider mites, making it exceptionally pest-resistant. Ideal for teas, cocktails, desserts, and fresh culinary applications.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Cherokee Trail of Tears
description: A sacred heirloom bean carried by Cherokee people during their forced relocation in the 1830s, preserving both history and exceptional flavor. These beautiful purple-black beans produce abundantly and are prized for their rich, meaty texture and deep, complex flavor that improves with cooking. A living piece of American history that connects modern gardeners to indigenous agricultural traditions.

name: Blue Lake Bush 274
description: The gold standard for green beans, developed from the famous Blue Lake pole variety but bred for compact bush growth. These tender, stringless pods have the classic fresh bean flavor and crisp texture that made Blue Lake beans the choice of commercial processors and home gardeners alike. Perfect for beginners who want restaurant-quality green beans without the hassle of trellising.
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, consistent moisture during flowering

sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 60°F. Plant 1 inch deep. Can succession plant every 2-3 weeks.

name: Kentucky Wonder Pole
description: America's most beloved heirloom pole bean, cherished by gardeners since the 1850s for its incredible productivity and old-fashioned bean flavor. These vigorous climbing vines produce an abundance of long, curved pods that are best harvested young and tender, though they can also be left to mature for excellent shell beans. A true garden workhorse that keeps producing until frost.

soil_type: Well-drained fertile soil, enriched with compost
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 65°F. Plant 1 inch deep near poles or trellises. Requires strong support.

flavor_profile: Rich, traditional bean flavor with meaty texture

common_pests: Bean beetles, spider mites, aphids
common_diseases: Rust, bacterial blight, anthracnose

name: Provider Bush
description: The ultimate beginner's green bean, specifically bred for reliability and performance in challenging growing conditions. This hardy bush variety tolerates cool weather better than most beans and produces heavy yields of tender, stringless pods even when other varieties struggle. Perfect for northern gardeners and anyone who wants a foolproof bean that delivers consistent results.
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, drought tolerant once established

name: Dragon Tongue
description: A stunning Dutch heirloom that transforms any garden into a conversation piece with its cream-colored pods dramatically streaked with purple flames. Beyond their striking appearance, these beans offer exceptional flavor when harvested young as snap beans, with a tender texture and sweet, nutty taste that's perfect fresh or lightly cooked. The purple markings fade when cooked, but the superior flavor remains.

soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 60°F. Plant 1-1.5 inches deep. Handle seeds gently as they can be delicate.

culinary_uses: Best fresh as snap beans, excellent in salads, light sautéing, steaming, specialty presentations

common_diseases: Bacterial blight, white mold, anthracnose

name: Scarlet Runner Bean
description: Scarlet flowers against green, heart-shaped foliage. Rapid climbers. Beans are edible and delicious when young. Keep pods picked for continuous bloom. May simply be called scarlet runner. A hummingbird favorite. Edible Flowers: The flowers offer a mild and sweet flavor. Use them to garnish or in salads, soups, and desserts.

soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil rich in organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil warms to 60°F and all danger of frost has passed. Plant 1-2 inches deep. Provide sturdy support structure.

shape: Flat, broad pods with kidney-shaped beans
flavor_profile: Fresh pods: tender and sweet. Mature beans: rich, nutty, and creamy
culinary_uses: Young pods steamed or sautéed, mature beans in stews, soups, and casseroles. Flowers are edible and colorful in salads.

name: Romano Italian Bush Bean
description: A classic Italian heirloom prized by chefs and home gardeners for its exceptionally wide, flat pods and rich, intense flavor. These meaty beans have a distinctly different taste and texture from regular green beans, with a more substantial bite and earthy flavor that holds up beautifully to cooking. Popular in Mediterranean cuisine, Romano beans are perfect for gardeners who want something special beyond ordinary green beans.

sowing_method: Direct sow 1-2 weeks after last frost when soil temperature reaches 60°F. Plant seeds 1 inch deep. Make successive plantings every 2-3 weeks.

culinary_uses: Sautéed with garlic and olive oil, braised in tomato sauce, grilled, or steamed. Excellent in Italian dishes and Mediterranean cooking.

name: Fortex Pole Bean
description: A French filet bean that produces extraordinarily long, slender pods up to 11 inches in length while maintaining tender texture and exceptional flavor. This vigorous climbing variety is beloved by gourmet gardeners for its elegant appearance and restaurant-quality taste that stays tender even at full size. Fortex offers an extended harvest window since the pods remain stringless and succulent much longer than typical varieties.

sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost when soil is 65°F+. Plant seeds 1 inch deep in hills.
shape: Round, slightly flattened with deep ribs
flavor_profile: Sweet, smooth, rich and creamy with classic pumpkin flavor
culinary_uses: Pies, breads, soups, roasting, purees, baking
disease_resistance: Good general disease tolerance
common_pests: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, vine borers
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, downy mildew

name: Kabocha Winter Squash
description: Often called Japanese pumpkin, this incredibly sweet winter squash has a texture like sweet potato and chestnut combined. The dense, bright orange flesh is naturally creamy and requires no butter or seasoning to be absolutely delicious. A favorite among chefs and health-conscious gardeners.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks after last frost when soil is warm. Start indoors 3-4 weeks before transplanting.
color: Dark green skin with orange flesh
shape: Round, squat, turban-like with deep ridges
flavor_profile: Extremely sweet, nutty, creamy like sweet potato with chestnut notes
culinary_uses: Roasting, tempura, curries, soups, desserts, purees
disease_resistance: Good resistance to common squash diseases
common_pests: Squash vine borer, cucumber beetles, squash bugs
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, black rot

name: Yellow Straightneck Zucchini
description: A prolific summer squash that produces straight, bright yellow fruits perfect for beginners and experienced gardeners alike. Unlike crookneck varieties, these stay straight making them easier to slice and store. The tender skin and mild flesh make them incredibly versatile in the kitchen.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with compost
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost when soil is 60°F+. Plant seeds 1 inch deep.
flavor_profile: Mild, tender, slightly nutty when young
culinary_uses: Grilling, sautéing, baking, bread, fritters, stir-fries
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to common viruses
common_pests: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, aphids
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, mosaic virus

name: Carnival Acorn Squash
description: A stunning hybrid that looks like a painter's masterpiece with cream-colored base decorated with green and orange stripes and splashes. Beyond its incredible beauty, it offers sweet, nutty flesh that's perfect for stuffing and the compact size is ideal for small families. This variety turns heads at farmers markets and dinner tables alike.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2 weeks after last frost. Plant seeds 1 inch deep in hills.
color: Cream base with green and orange stripes and splashes
flavor_profile: Sweet, nutty, slightly buttery with smooth texture
culinary_uses: Stuffing, roasting, baking, soups, side dishes
disease_resistance: Good powdery mildew resistance
common_pests: Squash vine borer, cucumber beetles, squash bugs
common_diseases: Bacterial wilt, black rot, downy mildew

name: Tromboncino Squash
description: An Italian heirloom that's part sculpture, part vegetable with its incredible curved neck resembling a trombone. This vigorous climbing vine can be eaten young like zucchini or left to mature into a sweet winter squash. Its unique growth habit and dual-purpose nature make it a fascinating conversation starter in any garden.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with compost
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil warms to 65°F. Provide strong trellis or allow to sprawl.
color: Light green when young, tan when mature
shape: Long curved neck with bulbous end, trombone-like
flavor_profile: Mild and tender when young, sweet and nutty when mature
culinary_uses: Young: like zucchini in stir-fries, grilling. Mature: roasting, soups, purees
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to squash vine borer, good disease tolerance
common_pests: Cucumber beetles, aphids, squash bugs
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, downy mildew

name: Chantenay Red Core Carrot
description: A robust French heirloom carrot perfect for heavy or shallow soils where longer varieties struggle. These broad-shouldered, conical carrots develop exceptional sweetness and a distinctive deep orange color throughout, making them ideal for fresh eating and storage. Their sturdy 5-6 inch length and excellent keeping quality have made them a favorite since the 1920s.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, deep consistent watering
soil_type: Deep, loose, sandy loam free of stones and clods
sowing_method: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in early spring, 2-3 weeks before last frost. Thin seedlings when 2 inches tall.
color: Deep orange throughout with bright orange core
size: 5-6 inches long, 2-3 inches wide at shoulder
flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, and tender with rich carrot flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, roasting, soups, stews, juicing, excellent for storage
disease_resistance: Good tolerance to splitting and cracking
common_pests: Carrot rust fly, wireworms, aphids
common_diseases: Alternaria leaf blight, bacterial soft rot, aster yellows

name: Watermelon Radish
description: Watermelon Radish is a visually striking heirloom root vegetable with a pale green exterior and distinctive pink-red flesh with white striations, resembling watermelon. Maturing in 90 days, this Japanese variety produces crisp, tender roots ideal for both fresh eating and cooking. The flesh offers a mild, sweet flavor with a subtle peppery finish and pleasant crispness that adds visual appeal and refreshing crunch to salads, slaws, and garnishes. This easy-to-grow variety thrives in deep, well-drained loam with full sun exposure.
soil_type: Deep, loose, well-drained loam
sowing_method: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in late summer for fall harvest, 10-12 weeks before hard frost. Can also plant in early spring.
color: White and pale green exterior with bright magenta interior
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet, and crisp with subtle peppery finish
culinary_uses: Fresh slicing for salads, pickling, roasting, garnishes, adds stunning color to dishes
disease_resistance: Good cold tolerance and slow to bolt
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggots, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, white rust

name: Hakurei Turnip
description: The smooth, flat-round, white roots mature early (just after radishes) and are best harvested young, up to 2" diameter. Eaten raw, the flavor is sweet and fruity and the texture is juicy and tender. The tops may be eaten raw or lightly cooked. Hakurei stays smooth as it sizes.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-8 hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in early spring or late summer. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest.
color: Pure white roots with bright green tops
flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, and mild with apple-like texture, no bitterness
culinary_uses: Excellent raw in salads, quick sautéing, roasting, pickling. Greens perfect for stir-fries and salads.
disease_resistance: Good bolt resistance and heat tolerance for a turnip
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggots, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, white rust

name: Storage #4 Rutabaga
description: The ultimate winter keeper rutabaga that actually improves in flavor during months of cold storage, developing incredible sweetness and creamy texture. This reliable variety produces large, uniform roots with classic purple shoulders and yellow flesh that becomes butter-smooth when cooked. A staple crop that can feed families through winter while providing exceptional nutrition and comfort-food satisfaction.
soil_type: Deep, well-drained, fertile loam with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in mid-summer, 12-14 weeks before hard frost. Plant after soil temperature drops below 75°F.
color: Purple-bronze shoulders with creamy yellow lower portion and deep yellow flesh
shape: Large, round to oval with taproot
flavor_profile: Sweet, nutty, and creamy when cooked, develops more sweetness after frost
culinary_uses: Mashing, roasting, soups, stews, casseroles. Excellent storage crop for winter meals.
disease_resistance: Excellent cold tolerance and storage ability
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggots, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, soft rot

name: Scarlet Nantes Carrot
description: A classic French heirloom carrot prized for its sweet flavor and crisp texture. These cylindrical orange roots grow 6-7 inches long with smooth skin and virtually no core, making them perfect for fresh eating and cooking. Their exceptional sweetness and tender flesh have made them the gold standard for home garden carrots worldwide.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, consistent moisture for straight roots
soil_type: Deep, loose, sandy loam free of rocks and debris
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Sow seeds ¼ inch deep in rows 12 inches apart. Thin seedlings when 2 inches tall.
flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, and tender with classic carrot flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, roasting, soups, stews, juicing, baby food
disease_resistance: Good general disease resistance, moderate carrot fly tolerance
common_pests: Carrot fly, wireworms, flea beetles, aphids
common_diseases: Leaf blight, black rot, cavity spot, carrot rust fly damage

name: Red Ace Beet
description: A reliable and adaptable standard red beet. Medium-tall, red-veined greens for bunching. Less uniform and not as attractive as our other red hybrids. Also available with NOP-compliant pelleting.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6 hours minimum)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, consistent moisture prevents woody texture
soil_type: Well-drained loam with organic matter, tolerates heavier soils
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost through early summer. Plant seeds ½ inch deep, thin to proper spacing when 3 inches tall.
color: Deep red with minimal white rings
flavor_profile: Sweet and earthy with mild, tender flesh
culinary_uses: Roasting, boiling, pickling, salads, juicing. Young greens excellent in salads and sautéed.
disease_resistance: Excellent downy mildew resistance, good cercospora leaf spot tolerance
common_pests: Leaf miners, flea beetles, aphids, wireworms
common_diseases: Cercospora leaf spot, downy mildew, damping-off

name: French Breakfast Radish
description: A well-maintained strain of this market staple. Longer than Nelson, with more white on the tip. Large healthy tops. After trying many strains we found this to be the best. NOTE: For longer, straighter roots, sow 15-20% more seeds per row 1/2 to 1 cm deeper, and do not irrigate unless absolutely necessary.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4+ hours)
water_requirement: ½ inch per week, consistent moisture prevents splitting
soil_type: Light, well-drained sandy loam, loose soil for best shape
sowing_method: Direct sow 4-6 weeks before last frost through fall. Plant ½ inch deep in rows 6 inches apart. Succession plant every 2 weeks.
color: Red top with white bottom and tip
shape: Oblong torpedo shape with blunt end
flavor_profile: Mild, crisp, and slightly peppery with sweet undertones
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, garnish, sandwiches, crudité platters, quick pickling
disease_resistance: Generally disease-free due to rapid growth cycle
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggot, aphids
common_diseases: Club root, black rot, white rust (rare due to quick maturity)

name: Purple Top White Globe Turnip
description: Selected strain of this popular Southern US variety. Smooth, round roots average 3-4" in diameter and are white below the soil line and bright purple above. Large lobed greens are suitable for cooking.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained loam, tolerates various soil types
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring or late summer for fall harvest. Plant seeds ½ inch deep, thin seedlings when 3 inches tall.
flavor_profile: Mild and sweet when young, becoming more peppery with size
culinary_uses: Roasting, mashing, soups, stews, pickling. Young greens excellent cooked or in salads.
disease_resistance: Good general resistance, tolerates clubroot better than related crops
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggot, aphids, cutworms
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, white rust, downy mildew

name: Chioggia Beet
description: Chioggia Beet is an Italian heirloom variety prized for its striking candy-striped interior of alternating red and white rings, revealed only when sliced. Maturing in approximately 55 days, these medium-sized round roots offer a distinctly sweeter, milder flavor profile with considerably less earthiness than standard beet varieties, making them excellent for both raw preparations and cooking. The vibrant, visually dramatic presentation makes Chioggia particularly popular in upscale culinary applications and farmers' markets. Plants thrive in full sun to partial shade with well-drained, organic-rich soil.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6 hours minimum)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, even moisture prevents white ring loss
soil_type: Well-drained loam with organic matter, avoid fresh manure
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost. Plant seeds ½ inch deep in rows 12 inches apart. Thin when seedlings are 3 inches tall.
color: Red and white concentric rings (candy cane striped)
flavor_profile: Sweet and mild with less earthy flavor than regular beets
culinary_uses: Best served raw or lightly cooked to preserve pattern. Excellent in salads, light roasting, pickling, garnish.
disease_resistance: Moderate disease resistance, similar to other beet varieties
common_pests: Leaf miners, flea beetles, aphids, wireworms
common_diseases: Cercospora leaf spot, downy mildew, scab, damping-off

name: Paris Market Carrot
description: A charming French heirloom perfect for containers and heavy soils where longer carrots struggle. These golf ball-sized roots are incredibly sweet and tender, making them ideal for gourmet cooking and an excellent choice for children's gardens due to their unique round shape.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained sandy loam, tolerates heavier soils better than long varieties
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring 2-3 weeks before last frost. Successive plantings every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest.

culinary_uses: Gourmet cooking, roasting whole, pickling, fresh eating, baby food
disease_resistance: Good resistance to splitting and cracking
common_pests: Carrot fly, aphids, wireworms
common_diseases: Leaf blight, black rot, cavity spot

name: Purple Daikon Radish
description: A stunning Asian variety that combines the mild, sweet flavor of traditional daikon with eye-catching purple and white coloring. These large radishes are perfect for both raw applications and cooking, offering gardeners something truly unique for their fall harvest.

sowing_method: Direct sow in late summer for fall harvest, 10-12 weeks before first frost. Spring sowings bolt quickly in heat.

culinary_uses: Pickling, stir-fries, soups, fresh slicing, fermentation, Asian dishes
disease_resistance: Good cold tolerance and disease resistance
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggot, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew

name: Nelson Carrot
description: Straight candy-red roots resist pithiness better than other long French types. Compared to French Breakfast, roots are shorter, wider, and show less white on the tips. Responds better to tight spacing. Improved top strength. NOTE: For longer, straighter roots, sow 15-20% more seeds per row 1/2 to 1 cm deeper, and do not irrigate unless absolutely necessary.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Deep, loose, well-drained sandy loam
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Make successive plantings every 2-3 weeks through summer for continuous harvest.
flavor_profile: Exceptionally sweet and crisp with fine texture and minimal core
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, juicing, cooking, storage, baby food, gourmet preparations
disease_resistance: Good resistance to leaf blight and cavity spot
common_pests: Carrot fly, aphids, wireworms
common_diseases: Alternaria leaf blight, bacterial soft rot

name: Golden Beet
description: Golden Beet is a vibrant heirloom root vegetable with brilliant yellow-gold skin and pale yellow flesh. Maturing in 75-90 days, it's prized for its distinctly sweet and mild flavor—far less earthy than red beets—with a tender, delicate texture. The golden hue alone sets it apart visually in the garden and kitchen. Golden Beets excel in fresh applications where their subtle sweetness shines: roasted as a side, thinly shaved into salads, or pickled. They're an excellent choice for gardeners seeking an easier-to-grow beet with broader culinary appeal and elegant presentation.
soil_type: Well-drained loam with organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost in spring, or late summer for fall harvest. Soak seeds overnight for better germination.
color: Bright golden yellow with green leaves
flavor_profile: Sweet and mild, less earthy than red beets with tender texture
culinary_uses: Roasting, salads, pickling, juicing, gourmet dishes, colorful vegetable medleys
disease_resistance: Good general disease resistance
common_pests: Leaf miners, aphids, flea beetles
common_diseases: Cercospora leaf spot, downy mildew, root rot

name: Danvers Half Long Carrot
description: A classic American heirloom carrot that has been a garden favorite since the 1870s. This reliable variety produces deep orange, broad-shouldered roots that taper to a blunt point, making them perfect for heavy or clay soils where longer carrots struggle. Known for exceptional sweetness and crisp texture that improves with cool weather.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, loose soil; tolerates heavier soils better than long varieties
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Sow seeds ¼ inch deep in rows 12-18 inches apart. Successive plantings every 2-3 weeks.
flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, and tender with classic carrot flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, cooking, roasting, juicing, storage. Excellent for soups and stews.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to cracking and splitting
common_pests: Carrot rust fly, aphids, wireworms
common_diseases: Alternaria leaf blight, bacterial soft rot, cavity spot

name: Purple Plum Radish
description: An eye-catching round radish with stunning deep purple skin and crisp white flesh that adds vibrant color to any garden or plate. This fast-growing variety maintains its mild, sweet flavor even as it reaches full size, making it perfect for gardeners who want beauty and taste. The golf ball-sized roots are as decorative as they are delicious.

water_requirement: ½-1 inch per week, consistent moisture
soil_type: Well-drained, loose soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost and again in late summer. Plant seeds ½ inch deep, thin to proper spacing.

flavor_profile: Mild, sweet, and crisp with less bite than traditional radishes
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, garnish, pickling. Beautiful when sliced to show purple and white contrast.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to clubroot and downy mildew
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage root maggots
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew

name: Bolero Carrot
description: Medium-long 7-8" roots are uniform, thick, slightly tapered, and blunt. Excellent eating quality both fresh and after long-term storage, with juicy texture and sweet, complex flavor. A great choice for fall and winter harvest. Tops are vigorous, fast, and healthy which makes for better field-holding ability and easier mechanical harvesting. Heavy Nantes type. Also available with NOP-compliant pelleting.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Deep, well-drained, loose soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Plant ¼ inch deep in rows 12 inches apart. Make successive plantings every 2-3 weeks.
flavor_profile: Exceptionally sweet and crunchy with smooth texture
culinary_uses: Premium fresh eating, juicing, cooking, baby carrots when harvested young
disease_resistance: Alternaria Blight (High); Bacterial Blight (Intermediate); Cavity Spot (Intermediate); Cercospora Blight (Intermediate); Powdery Mildew (High)

name: Early Wonder Tall Top Beet
description: Early Wonder is harvested in bunches with large, vigorous tops attached to the young beets. Tall, bright, glossy green, red-veined tops and slightly flattened red roots. Excellent foliar disease resistance for reliably clean bunches.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-8 hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost. Plant seeds ½ inch deep, thin seedlings to proper spacing. Fall plantings 10-12 weeks before hard frost.
color: Deep red roots with bright green tops tinged red
flavor_profile: Sweet, earthy roots with tender, mild-flavored greens
culinary_uses: Roots for roasting, boiling, pickling. Greens excellent for salads and sautéing. Both can be harvested young.
disease_resistance: Good tolerance to leaf spot diseases
common_pests: Leaf miners, aphids, flea beetles
common_diseases: Cercospora leaf spot, downy mildew, damping off

name: Tokyo Cross Turnip
description: An award-winning Japanese hybrid turnip that revolutionized home turnip growing with its incredible speed and sweet, mild flavor. This All-America Selections winner produces perfectly white, smooth roots in just over a month, with crisp texture that never gets woody or strong-tasting. The compact size and quick maturity make it perfect for succession planting and small gardens.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-8 hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost and again in late summer. Plant seeds ¼ inch deep, thin to proper spacing when 2 inches tall.
color: Pure white roots with bright green tops
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild, and crisp with no bitter aftertaste
culinary_uses: Excellent raw in salads, quick cooking, stir-fries, roasting. Greens are tender and edible.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to clubroot and turnip mosaic virus
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage root maggots
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, white rust

name: Detroit Dark Red
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Detroit Dark Red'
description: A classic heirloom cabbage prized for its deep burgundy-red color and exceptional storage capabilities. This reliable variety produces dense, round heads with crisp texture and sweet flavor that intensifies after light frosts. Perfect for both fresh eating and traditional fermented preparations like sauerkraut.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost for spring crop, or direct sow in mid to late summer for fall harvest.

culinary_uses: Fresh slaws, braising, sauerkraut, pickling, roasting. Excellent for fermentation.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to splitting, moderate clubroot tolerance
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cutworms
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew, alternaria leaf spot

name: Romanesco
description: Romanesco is a striking Italian heirloom brassica featuring distinctive spiraling, lime-green heads composed of fractal-like florets that resemble miniature trees. Maturing in approximately 95 days, it bridges broccoli and cauliflower with a sophisticated nutty-sweet flavor profile and tender-crisp texture. Its mild brassica notes and delicate taste distinguish it from heartier cruciferous varieties, making it exceptional for both raw preparations and light cooking. Visually compelling and culinarily refined, Romanesco commands attention on any plate while remaining approachable for gardeners willing to manage its moderate to challenging cultivation requirements.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high nitrogen content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost for spring crop, or start in mid-summer for fall harvest. Transplant carefully to avoid root disturbance.
flavor_profile: Nutty and sweet with mild brassica notes, tender-crisp texture
culinary_uses: Roasting, steaming, raw in salads, pasta dishes, gourmet presentations. Showcase variety for special occasions.
disease_resistance: Moderate disease resistance, sensitive to temperature fluctuations
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, diamondback moths
common_diseases: Clubroot, downy mildew, black rot, bacterial soft rot

name: Lacinato Kale
description: Also known as 'Dinosaur Kale' or 'Tuscan Kale', this Italian heirloom features distinctive dark blue-green leaves with a pebbled texture resembling reptilian skin. The narrow, strap-like leaves have a sweeter, more tender flavor than curly kale and become incredibly sweet after frost exposure. An absolute must-have for the health-conscious gardener seeking both nutrition and gourmet flavor.

water_requirement: 1 inch per week, drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poor soils
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost for spring crop, or late summer for fall/winter harvest. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks.

flavor_profile: Sweet, mild, and tender with less bitterness than curly kale
culinary_uses: Salads, smoothies, sautéing, soups, chips, massaged raw preparations. Excellent for juicing.
disease_resistance: Excellent cold tolerance, good disease resistance overall
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, leaf miners
common_diseases: Clubroot, downy mildew, alternaria leaf spot

name: Golden Acre Cabbage
description: A compact, early-maturing cabbage that's perfect for small gardens and beginning gardeners seeking reliable results. This variety produces perfectly round, solid heads with sweet, crisp leaves and has the advantage of maturing quickly without taking up excessive garden space. An excellent choice for succession planting and container growing.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow in early spring. For fall crop, start in mid-summer.
flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, and mild with tender leaves
culinary_uses: Fresh slaws, steaming, stir-fries, stuffed cabbage, soups. Excellent for fresh eating.
disease_resistance: Good splitting resistance, moderate disease tolerance
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, root maggots
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew, fusarium yellows

name: De Cicco Broccoli
description: A traditional Italian heirloom variety producing small, avg. 3-4", main heads projected well above the foliage followed by a large yield of side-shoot spears. Nonuniform in maturity, resulting in a long harvest period. Grow as later succession to BC1611. Longer harvest period than other sprouting broccoli varieties, with more variation in shoot size. For spring or fall production. Pinching recommended to encourage heavier side-shoot production. Unsized seed.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter and nitrogen
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost for spring crop, or direct sow in late summer for fall harvest. Transplant after hardening off.
shape: Compact central head with numerous side shoots
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender, and mild with excellent texture
culinary_uses: Steaming, stir-fries, fresh eating, soups, freezing. Perfect for continuous harvest cooking.
disease_resistance: Good heat tolerance for broccoli, moderate disease resistance
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, diamondback moths
common_diseases: Clubroot, downy mildew, black rot, alternaria leaf spot

name: Black Magic Kale
description: Black Magic is a well-maintained selection of Toscano kale with long, narrow leaves for attractive and tall, straight bunches. Dark blue-green leaves with beautiful savoy. Lacinato or "dinosaur" type kale.
