soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last spring frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks early. For fall crop, sow 12-14 weeks before first fall frost.
shape: Deeply curled and savoyed with ruffled edges
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild, and tender with a slight peppery finish
culinary_uses: Salads, smoothies, sautéing, roasting, kale chips, soups and stews
disease_resistance: Good resistance to downy mildew and black rot
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage loopers
common_diseases: Clubroot, black leg, white rust, downy mildew

name: Premium Crop Broccoli
description: An award-winning F1 hybrid that produces large, dense blue-green heads with exceptional flavor and texture. This reliable variety offers excellent heat tolerance and produces abundant side shoots after the main head is harvested. A favorite among both home gardeners and commercial growers for its consistent performance.

soil_type: Fertile, well-drained soil rich in organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. For fall crop, start 12-14 weeks before first fall frost.

flavor_profile: Sweet, nutty flavor with tender stems and florets
culinary_uses: Steaming, roasting, stir-frying, fresh in salads, freezing, soups
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to downy mildew and black rot
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage root maggots

name: Early Jersey Wakefield Cabbage

The pointed heads are distinctively attractive and space-efficient.

These small to medium roots mature in just 37 days, making them quick from seed to harvest. The mild, sweet flesh develops additional complexity after frost, becoming noticeably sweeter. Young roots offer a subtle peppery bite, while mature roots are tender and suitable for roasting, mashing, or raw consumption. The purple coloring is both ornamental and traditional, making this an excellent choice for gardeners seeking reliable, flavorful turnips with minimal fuss.

In spring or fall, sow alone or mixed with vetch and/or oats, winter rye, or ryegrass. Harvest for grain at 16-18% moisture or when the seeds are hardened and fully mature. Inoculate with Garden Combination Inoculant (#7531) or Pea, Vetch, and Lentil Inoculant (#7534) for best performance.

Rich in vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants, sunflower microgreens are a popular choice for health-conscious growers seeking nutritious homegrown greens with minimal effort.

The microgreen format provides nutrient density and visual appeal in minimal space, thriving under moderate light or grow lights. Easy to grow under moderate to bright light with minimal pest pressure, radish microgreens are popular among home and commercial growers seeking quick harvests with maximum flavor impact.

Chia microgreens have delicate, tender leaves with a mild, slightly tangy flavor that's completely different from the crunchy seeds. They're packed with omega-3 fatty acids and provide a gourmet touch to salads and smoothies.

These heirloom microgreens feature vibrant green cotyledons with a distinctive intense lemon scent and bright, citrusy flavor with subtle minty undertones. They mature quickly in well-draining growing mediums under 4-6+ hours of light daily. The defining characteristic is their potent lemon aromatics that intensify with age, making them ideal for finishing salads, beverages, and desserts. Moderate to advanced difficulty level due to moisture management requirements and susceptibility to aphids and spider mites in dry conditions.

These microgreens deliver a mild, cabbage-like flavor with a subtle peppery bite and satisfying crunch. The striking purple coloration makes them an excellent garnish for salads, grain bowls, and plated dishes, while their tender texture works well in smoothies and fresh preparations. Easy to grow in standard seed starting mixes with consistent moisture and indirect light, they're ideal for beginners exploring microgreen cultivation.

sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost when soil is 65°F or warmer. Plant 1-2 inches deep. Requires longer season than modern varieties.
shape: Narrow ears with small kernels in irregular, zigzag pattern (no rows)
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender, and creamy with traditional corn flavor and fine texture
culinary_uses: Excellent for cream-style corn, soups, succotash. Good fresh eating and perfect for traditional corn recipes.
disease_resistance: Limited disease resistance typical of heirlooms. Susceptible to most common corn diseases.
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, birds
common_diseases: Common rust, smut, northern corn leaf blight, Stewart's wilt

name: Bodacious
description: True to its bold name, Bodacious delivers outstanding performance with remarkable heat tolerance and disease resistance that makes it ideal for challenging growing conditions. This reliable hybrid produces consistently sweet, yellow ears even when temperatures soar, making it a favorite among Southern gardeners and anyone dealing with hot summers. Its robust nature and dependable yields prove that great corn can thrive even in tough conditions.
plant_spacing: 6-8 inches apart in rows 30-36 inches apart
soil_type: Adaptable to various soil types, prefers well-drained fertile soil
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil temperature reaches 60°F. Plant 1-1.5 inches deep. Excellent heat tolerance allows later plantings.
shape: Well-filled cylindrical ears with 16-18 straight rows
flavor_profile: Sweet and tender with classic corn flavor, maintains quality in heat
culinary_uses: Versatile for all fresh uses - boiling, grilling, roasting. Excellent processing variety for freezing and canning.
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to Stewart's wilt and common rust. Good resistance to northern corn leaf blight and gray leaf spot.
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, lesser cornstalk borer
common_diseases: Occasionally affected by smut, resistant to most major corn diseases

name: Glass Gem
description: A distinctive presentation in fall displays, the glassy-looking kernels are unique to this variety. Kernels can be popped, but results may vary. Sturdy plants can reach 10' tall. Avg. ear size 6-8". Requires a fairly long growing season for favorable yields. Bred by the late Carl Barnes, an Oklahoma Cherokee who dedicated his career to reclaiming and preserving seed of traditional Native American corns.
plant_spacing: 8-12 inches apart in rows 36-42 inches apart
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poor soils better than sweet corn
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 60°F. Plant 1-2 inches deep. Requires full season to mature for best colors.
color: Multicolored - blue, red, yellow, white, pink, orange, purple in various combinations
shape: Tapered ears with 8-12 rows of translucent, glass-like kernels
flavor_profile: Not eaten fresh - meant for grinding into cornmeal or ornamental use
culinary_uses: Grinding into colorful cornmeal, polenta, decoration. Primarily ornamental and cultural preservation.
disease_resistance: Good natural resistance typical of flint corns. More resistant than sweet corn varieties.
common_pests: Corn earworm, birds (attracted to colorful kernels), European corn borer
common_diseases: Common rust, smut, northern corn leaf blight (generally more resistant than sweet corn)

name: Golden Bantam
description: A true heirloom classic that's been delighting gardeners since 1902, Golden Bantam is the corn variety that set the standard for sweet corn flavor. This compact variety produces tender, golden kernels with an intense, old-fashioned corn taste that modern hybrids try to emulate. Perfect for small gardens and those who want to grow a piece of American agricultural history.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil temperature reaches 60°F and all danger of frost has passed. Plant in blocks for better pollination.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 8-12 rows of kernels
flavor_profile: Rich, intensely sweet with classic corn flavor and tender texture
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, corn on the cob, freezing, creamed corn, corn salads
disease_resistance: Limited disease resistance, susceptible to common corn smut and bacterial wilt
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, raccoons, birds
common_diseases: Common smut, bacterial wilt, northern corn leaf blight, rust

name: Peaches and Cream
scientific_name: Zea mays var. saccharata 'Peaches and Cream'
description: This stunning bicolor variety produces ears with a beautiful mix of white and yellow kernels that look like peaches and cream. Known for its exceptional sweetness and tender texture, this hybrid variety stays sweet longer than many other corns after picking. The reliable production and gorgeous appearance make it a favorite for both home gardens and farmers markets.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil rich in organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil temperature is consistently 60°F or higher. Plant in blocks of at least 4 rows for proper pollination.
flavor_profile: Exceptionally sweet and tender with a creamy texture and mild corn flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, corn salads, freezing, corn chowder
disease_resistance: Good resistance to northern corn leaf blight and rust
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, Japanese beetles, raccoons
common_diseases: Stewart's bacterial wilt, common smut, southern corn leaf blight

name: Stowell's Evergreen
description: Dating back to 1848, Stowell's Evergreen earned its name for staying tender and sweet longer than other varieties, remaining in perfect eating condition for weeks. This tall, vigorous heirloom produces large ears with deep white kernels and is considered one of the finest varieties for canning and preserving. Its reliable performance and extended harvest window make it invaluable for serious corn growers.
soil_type: Deep, fertile, well-drained soil with high organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 65°F for best germination. Requires long growing season. Plant in blocks for optimal pollination.
shape: Large, well-filled ears with 14-20 rows of deep kernels
flavor_profile: Sweet and tender with excellent flavor that holds quality longer than most varieties
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, canning, freezing, cream-style corn, excellent for preservation
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to common smut, susceptible to bacterial diseases
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, fall armyworm
common_diseases: Bacterial wilt, northern corn leaf blight, gray leaf spot, rust

name: Incredible
description: Living up to its name, Incredible is a supersweet hybrid that produces consistently large ears packed with exceptionally sweet, tender kernels. This reliable performer offers excellent disease resistance and strong stalks that resist lodging, making it perfect for areas with challenging weather. The sugar content remains high even days after harvest, giving home gardeners more flexibility in their harvest timing.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good moisture retention
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil temperature reaches 60°F. Can be succession planted every 2 weeks for extended harvest.
shape: Cylindrical, well-filled ears with 16-18 straight rows
flavor_profile: Extremely sweet and tender with crisp texture and intense corn flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, corn salads, freezing, raw in salads when young
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to northern corn leaf blight, rust, and bacterial wilt
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, birds, raccoons
common_diseases: Common smut, gray leaf spot, southern rust

name: Ruby Queen
description: This stunning ornamental and culinary corn produces ears with deep ruby-red kernels that look like precious gems. While primarily grown for decoration and grinding into colorful cornmeal, the young ears can also be eaten fresh when harvested early. Ruby Queen adds dramatic color to fall displays while providing a unique ingredient for adventurous home cooks and bakers.
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poorer soils than sweet corn varieties
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil warms to 60°F. Allow full maturation and drying on stalk for best color development.
color: Deep ruby red to burgundy kernels
shape: Tapered ears with 12-16 rows of hard, flint-type kernels
flavor_profile: Nutty, corn flavor when ground; sweet when harvested young and fresh
culinary_uses: Ornamental displays, grinding into red cornmeal, polenta, tortillas, young ears can be eaten fresh
disease_resistance: Good natural resistance to most corn diseases, hardy variety
common_pests: Birds, corn earworm, European corn borer, squirrels
common_diseases: Common smut, northern corn leaf blight, rust

name: Early Sunglow
description: A reliable early-season sweet corn that delivers impressive flavor despite its quick maturity. This variety produces tender, golden kernels with excellent sweetness that holds well after harvest. Perfect for northern gardeners with shorter seasons or anyone wanting fresh corn on the table quickly.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2 weeks after last frost when soil temperature reaches 60°F. Plant in blocks for better pollination.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 12-14 rows of kernels
flavor_profile: Sweet and tender with classic corn flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, boiling, roasting. Good for freezing when harvested at peak sweetness.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to northern corn leaf blight and rust
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, raccoons
common_diseases: Southern corn leaf blight, common smut, corn rust

name: Painted Mountain
description: Developed for its hardiness, earliness, drought tolerance, and nutritional density in the mountains of Montana. Avg. 6-7" multicolor ears for roasting or grinding into high-nutrition flour or hominy grits. Bred by Dave Christensen of Big Timber, MT from multiple Native American heirlooms. Avg. 4' plants.
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poor soils better than sweet corn
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 2-3 weeks before transplanting. Very cold tolerant.
color: Multi-colored kernels in red, blue, yellow, white, purple, and orange
shape: Tapered ears with 8-12 rows of mixed-color kernels
flavor_profile: Nutty, earthy flavor when ground into flour or meal
culinary_uses: Grinding into flour for bread, tortillas, polenta. Excellent for popping corn and decorative uses.
disease_resistance: Excellent cold tolerance and good disease resistance overall
common_pests: Corn earworm, birds, raccoons, squirrels
common_diseases: Generally disease resistant, occasional rust in humid conditions

name: Sugar Enhanced (SE) Butter and Sugar
description: A beloved bicolor sweet corn that combines the best of yellow and white kernels on the same ear. This sugar-enhanced variety offers excellent flavor that's sweeter than standard corn but not as intense as supersweets, with kernels that stay tender longer after harvest. The attractive yellow and white pattern makes it as beautiful as it is delicious.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with high organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks after last frost when soil is consistently 65°F. Plant in blocks for optimal pollination.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 16-18 rows of mixed kernels
flavor_profile: Sweet and creamy with balanced corn flavor, tender texture
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, steaming, freezing. Excellent for corn salads and summer dishes.
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to northern corn leaf blight
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, birds
common_diseases: Stewart's wilt, common rust, gray leaf spot

name: Jubilee
description: A time-tested yellow sweet corn that has been a garden favorite since the 1960s for its reliable performance and excellent flavor. This variety produces large, well-filled ears with tender, sweet kernels that have just the right balance of sweetness and corn taste. Jubilee is known for its consistent germination and strong disease resistance, making it perfect for both beginners and experienced gardeners.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with plenty of compost
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks after last frost when soil temperature is 60-65°F. Plant successive crops every 2 weeks.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 18-20 rows of plump kernels
flavor_profile: Sweet and tender with rich, full corn flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, boiling, roasting. Excellent for canning and freezing.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to bacterial wilt and northern corn leaf blight
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, raccoons
common_diseases: Southern corn leaf blight, common rust, gray leaf spot

name: Ambrosia
description: A supersweet bicolor corn that lives up to its heavenly name with incredibly tender, crisp kernels that burst with sweetness. This variety maintains its sugar content much longer than standard sweet corn, staying sweet for days after harvest when properly stored. The striking yellow and white kernel pattern makes it as visually appealing as it is delicious on the dinner table.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with high fertility
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil is 65-70°F, or start indoors. Requires isolation from other corn types by 250 feet or 2 weeks.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 16-18 rows of crisp kernels
flavor_profile: Exceptionally sweet and crisp with intense corn flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, steaming. Best eaten fresh but excellent for freezing at peak sweetness.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to northern corn leaf blight and common rust
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, birds, raccoons
common_diseases: Stewart's wilt, gray leaf spot, southern corn leaf blight

name: Silver King
description: A stunning white sweet corn that produces large, tender ears filled with plump, milky-white kernels bursting with classic corn sweetness. This reliable heirloom variety has been a garden favorite since the 1950s, delivering consistent harvests of beautiful ears that are perfect for fresh eating, freezing, or canning. Silver King's vigorous stalks and excellent ear fill make it an ideal choice for gardeners wanting dependable white corn with old-fashioned flavor.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with high organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 60°F and all danger of frost has passed. Plant in blocks for better pollination.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 14-16 rows of kernels
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender, and milky with classic corn flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, boiling, grilling, freezing, canning, corn salads
disease_resistance: Good general disease tolerance, resistant to Stewart's wilt
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, cutworms
common_diseases: Common rust, northern corn leaf blight, smut

name: Honey 'N Pearl
description: A premium triplesweet hybrid that delivers extraordinary sweetness and crisp texture that holds for days after harvest. This bicolor variety produces consistently large ears with tender kernels that convert sugars slowly, maintaining their incredible taste longer than standard varieties. The perfect choice for gardeners who want restaurant-quality corn from their backyard.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil rich in organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil temperature reaches 60°F. Plant in blocks of at least 4 rows for optimal pollination.
flavor_profile: Super sweet and tender with excellent eating quality
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, boiling, roasting, corn on the cob, salads
disease_resistance: Good resistance to northern corn leaf blight and common rust
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, fall armyworm, aphids
common_diseases: Stewart's wilt, gray leaf spot, common smut

name: Trinity
description: A premium tricolor sweet corn that creates stunning ears with white, yellow, and red kernels in beautiful random patterns, making each ear a unique work of art. Beyond its striking appearance, Trinity delivers exceptional sweetness and tender texture that rivals the best hybrid varieties. This newer variety combines the visual appeal of ornamental corn with the eating quality that sweet corn lovers demand, perfect for gardeners who want both beauty and flavor.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil warms to 65°F. Isolate from other corn varieties by 250+ feet or stagger planting times.
color: Tricolor - white, yellow, and red kernels
shape: Well-filled ears with 12-14 rows in random color patterns
flavor_profile: Very sweet and tender with excellent fresh eating quality
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, grilling, decorative presentations, farmers market sales
disease_resistance: Good general disease resistance, tolerant to common rust
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, raccoons
common_diseases: Northern corn leaf blight, Stewart's wilt, common smut

name: How Sweet It Is
description: This pure white supersweet corn lives up to its name with incredibly tender kernels that practically melt in your mouth and sweetness that stays fresh for days after harvest. The compact 6-foot plants are perfect for smaller gardens while still producing full-sized ears with excellent tip fill and uniformity. How Sweet It Is has become a favorite among home gardeners who want maximum sweetness in a manageable plant size that's easy to grow and harvest.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with high organic matter content
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil is 65°F or warmer. Supersweet varieties need warm soil for good germination. Isolate from other corn types.
shape: Cylindrical ears with 16-18 rows of plump kernels
flavor_profile: Exceptionally sweet and tender, maintains sweetness longer than standard varieties
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, corn on the cob, salads, freezing for winter use
disease_resistance: Resistant to Stewart's wilt, moderate resistance to rust diseases
common_pests: Corn earworm, Japanese beetles, corn borer, aphids
common_diseases: Northern corn leaf blight, gray leaf spot, crazy top

name: Mirai
description: A revolutionary yellow sweet corn hybrid that represents the cutting edge of corn breeding, combining incredible tenderness with sweetness that intensifies rather than diminishes after harvest. Mirai produces beautiful golden ears with exceptional kernel quality and extended harvest window, staying tender and sweet longer than traditional varieties. This Japanese-developed variety has quickly gained popularity among discerning gardeners who want the absolute best in sweet corn technology and flavor.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with excellent fertility
sowing_method: Direct sow when soil reaches 60°F. Plant in blocks for best pollination. Isolate from other corn varieties.
shape: Uniform cylindrical ears with 16-20 rows of plump kernels
flavor_profile: Ultra-sweet and creamy with incredible tenderness and extended eating quality
culinary_uses: Premium fresh eating, gourmet preparations, grilling, raw in salads
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to northern corn leaf blight and rust diseases
common_pests: Corn earworm, European corn borer, corn rootworm, birds
common_diseases: Stewart's wilt, gray leaf spot, southern corn leaf blight

name: Heritage Red Raspberry
description: America's most popular everbearing raspberry variety, delivering two harvests per year with exceptional reliability. These medium-sized berries offer outstanding flavor and freeze beautifully, making them perfect for both fresh eating and preserving. Heritage is renowned for its disease resistance and ability to produce consistent crops even for novice gardeners.
days_to_germination: 14-28 (from seed, though typically grown from canes)
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil rich in organic matter
sowing_method: Plant dormant canes in early spring or fall. Space in rows with support system. Remove weak canes and maintain 4-6 strong canes per plant.
shape: Classic raspberry cone shape with drupelets
flavor_profile: Sweet-tart balance with classic raspberry flavor, excellent fresh or processed
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, jams, jellies, baking, freezing, smoothies, desserts
disease_resistance: Good resistance to root rot and many common raspberry diseases
common_pests: Japanese beetle, raspberry cane borer, spider mites, aphids
common_diseases: Gray mold, powdery mildew, verticillium wilt, cane blight

name: Duke Blueberry
description: One of the finest early-season blueberry varieties, producing large, firm berries with exceptional sweet flavor in mid-June. Duke offers reliable annual production and excellent cold hardiness, making it a favorite among northern gardeners. The upright, vigorous bushes also provide beautiful white spring flowers and stunning orange-red fall foliage.
days_to_germination: 30-60 (from seed, though typically grown from plants)
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Acidic, well-drained, organic-rich soil
sowing_method: Plant container-grown bushes in spring or fall. Mulch heavily with acidic materials like pine needles. Cross-pollinate with other blueberry varieties for best fruit set.
color: Medium to dark blue with light bloom
shape: Round to slightly flattened, classic blueberry
flavor_profile: Sweet with mild tartness, excellent fresh eating quality
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, muffins, pancakes, pies, jams, freezing excellent
disease_resistance: Good resistance to mummy berry and moderate resistance to powdery mildew
common_pests: Birds, aphids, scale insects, blueberry maggot
common_diseases: Mummy berry, powdery mildew, root rot, leaf spot

name: Chester Thornless Blackberry
description: The premier thornless blackberry variety, offering large, sweet berries without the painful thorns that make harvesting difficult. Chester produces heavy crops of firm, flavorful berries in late summer that are perfect for fresh eating and hold their shape beautifully in pies and jams. This semi-erect variety combines exceptional productivity with excellent disease resistance and cold hardiness.
days_to_germination: 21-35 (from seed, though typically grown from canes)
water_requirement: 1-2 inches per week, deep watering during fruit development
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Plant dormant canes in early spring. Provide trellis support for semi-erect growth. Prune out old canes after fruiting and thin new canes to 4-6 per plant.
flavor_profile: Sweet with mild tartness, rich blackberry flavor, firm texture
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, pies, cobblers, jams, jellies, wine making, freezing
disease_resistance: Excellent resistance to orange rust and crown gall
common_pests: Japanese beetle, aphids, spider mites, spotted wing drosophila
common_diseases: Anthracnose, gray mold, powdery mildew, cane blight

name: Honeyberry (Haskap)
description: An exciting superfruit that's gaining popularity among adventurous gardeners, producing unique elongated blue berries with a complex flavor reminiscent of blueberry-raspberry-cherry. These extremely cold-hardy bushes are among the first fruits to ripen in early summer, often ready weeks before strawberries. Honeyberries thrive in northern climates where other berries struggle and offer exceptional antioxidant content.
days_to_germination: 30-90 (from seed, though typically grown from plants)
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, tolerates some drought once established
soil_type: Well-drained, organic-rich soil, very adaptable

color: Dark blue to purple with blue-white bloom
shape: Oblong to cylindrical, unique among berries
flavor_profile: Complex sweet-tart with hints of blueberry, raspberry, and cherry
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, smoothies, jams, baking, wine making, excellent frozen
disease_resistance: Excellent natural disease resistance, very few pest problems
common_pests: Birds (main concern), aphids occasionally
common_diseases: Very disease resistant, occasional powdery mildew in humid conditions

name: Jewel Black Raspberry
description: A premium black raspberry variety developed by Cornell University, prized for its exceptional sweet-tart flavor and firm, glossy berries. Jewel produces abundant crops of large, jet-black fruits that hold their shape beautifully for fresh eating or baking. This hardy variety offers excellent disease resistance and reliable harvests that make it a favorite among home gardeners.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with high organic matter
sowing_method: Plant bare root canes in early spring or fall. Set crown at soil level and water thoroughly.
shape: Round aggregate berry with hollow core
flavor_profile: Rich, sweet-tart with intense berry flavor and wine-like complexity
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, jams, jellies, pies, cobblers, smoothies, wine making
disease_resistance: Good resistance to anthracnose and powdery mildew
common_pests: Japanese beetles, raspberry crown borer, aphids, spider mites
common_diseases: Cane blight, verticillium wilt, root rot in poorly drained soils

name: Elliott Blueberry
description: The season extender that blueberry enthusiasts dream about, Elliott produces sweet, firm berries well into late summer when other varieties have finished. This vigorous highbush variety offers excellent cold hardiness and consistent heavy yields of medium-large berries with exceptional storage quality. Its extended harvest period makes it invaluable for home gardeners wanting fresh blueberries from July through September.

soil_type: Acidic, well-drained soil high in organic matter
sowing_method: Plant container-grown bushes in spring or fall. Dig holes twice the width of root ball, amend with peat moss.

flavor_profile: Sweet with mild tartness, firm texture and excellent fresh eating quality
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, muffins, pancakes, pies, jams, freezing exceptionally well
disease_resistance: Good resistance to mummy berry and stem canker
common_pests: Blueberry maggot, aphids, scale insects, birds during harvest
common_diseases: Anthracnose, phytophthora root rot, bacterial canker

name: Triple Crown Blackberry
description: Living up to its royal name, Triple Crown delivers the perfect trifecta of thornless canes, exceptional fruit quality, and outstanding disease resistance. This semi-erect variety produces large, sweet blackberries with seeds so small they're barely noticeable, making every bite pure pleasure. Developed at the University of Arkansas, it's become the go-to choice for gardeners who want premium blackberries without the painful thorns.

common_diseases: Cane blight, verticillium wilt, rosette disease in some regions

name: Northland Blueberry
description: A cold-hardy, half-high blueberry bush that's perfect for northern gardeners and smaller spaces. This reliable variety produces abundant crops of medium-sized, sweet berries and offers beautiful fall color, making it both productive and ornamental in the landscape.

soil_type: Acidic, well-drained soil high in organic matter

flavor_profile: Sweet with mild tartness and classic blueberry flavor

common_pests: Blueberry maggot, aphids, scale insects, birds
common_diseases: Mummy berry, anthracnose, powdery mildew

name: Natchez Thornless Blackberry
description: The earliest ripening thornless blackberry variety, producing huge, sweet berries up to two weeks before other cultivars. Developed by the University of Arkansas, Natchez offers exceptional flavor, impressive berry size, and vigorous disease-resistant canes that make harvesting a pleasure without thorns.

soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter

culinary_uses: Fresh eating, pies, jams, smoothies, baking, freezing

name: Patriot Blueberry
description: An exceptionally hardy northern highbush blueberry known for its consistent heavy yields and superior cold tolerance. This vigorous variety produces large, flavorful berries early in the season and demonstrates excellent resistance to root rot, making it ideal for challenging growing conditions and northern climates.

name: Emerald Blueberry
description: A revolutionary southern highbush blueberry that produces massive, sweet berries without requiring winter chill hours. Perfect for warm climates where traditional blueberries struggle, Emerald offers exceptional fruit quality with a long harvest season that extends from late spring through early summer.

soil_type: Acidic, well-draining soil rich in organic matter

common_pests: Birds, aphids, scale insects, spotted wing drosophila

name: Everbearing Red Raspberry
description: A prolific everbearing raspberry that delivers two harvests per year - a summer crop on second-year canes and a fall crop on first-year canes. Known for producing large, firm berries with excellent flavor and superior keeping quality, making it a favorite among home gardeners seeking extended harvests.

name: Prime-Ark Freedom Blackberry
description: A game-changing primocane blackberry that produces two crops annually on thornless canes, combining the best of both worlds - convenience and productivity. This University of Arkansas release offers large, firm berries with excellent flavor and the unique advantage of fruiting on first-year canes, extending your harvest season significantly.
water_requirement: 1-2 inches per week, consistent moisture during fruiting
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Plant bare-root canes or potted plants in early spring. Provide sturdy trellis system for support.

culinary_uses: Fresh eating, cobblers, pies, jams, smoothies, excellent for freezing
disease_resistance: Good resistance to anthracnose and orange rust
common_pests: Spotted wing drosophila, aphids, spider mites, cane borers
common_diseases: Gray mold, anthracnose, cane blight, double blossom

name: Sugar Snap
description: Johnny's superior strain. From an ongoing collaboration with the family of the late Dr. Calvin Lamborn, the breeder of Sugar Snap. A household name for its sweet, crisp pods, Sugar Snap germinates well in cold, wet soil, and the vigorous plants quickly produce a thick stand that requires support. Vines avg. 72" with 1 or 2 pods per node. Yields well over a long season in both hot and cold weather. Remove string from pods before cooking. Needs trellising. AAS Winner. Also available in organic seed.

water_requirement: 1 inch per week, consistent moisture especially during flowering
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil enriched with compost
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost date. Plant 1-2 inches deep. Provide tall trellises or supports.

flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, and juicy with concentrated pea flavor

common_diseases: Powdery mildew, downy mildew, fusarium wilt, root rot

description: A compact heirloom shelling pea perfect for small gardens and beginning gardeners since 1908. Connecticut Field is the quintessential pumpkin variety that connects modern gardeners to centuries of American agricultural tradition.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter content
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil temperature reaches 65°F. Plant seeds 1-2 inches deep in hills. Can start indoors 2-3 weeks before transplanting.
shape: Round to slightly flattened with deep ribbing
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild pumpkin flavor with smooth texture when cooked
culinary_uses: Jack-o'-lantern carving, pumpkin pie, roasted seeds, soups, breads, and traditional fall baking
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance typical of heirloom varieties
common_pests: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, squash vine borers
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, black rot, downy mildew

description: A superior acorn squash variety developed by Burpee in the 1890s, prized for its consistent acorn shape and exceptional sweet, nutty flavor. Fordhook produces thick-walled fruits with bright orange flesh that becomes incredibly tender and flavorful when roasted. This heirloom variety offers better eating quality than many modern acorn squashes and stores exceptionally well through winter.

common_pests: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, squash vine borers
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, downy mildew

name: Gem Squash
description: A unique South African heirloom that produces small, round squashes perfect for individual servings and container growing. These charming little squashes start cream-colored and develop beautiful golden-yellow skin at maturity, with sweet, tender flesh that's delicious when simply steamed or roasted whole. Gem squash is prized for its compact size, attractive appearance, and excellent storage capabilities.

sowing_method: Direct sow after soil temperature reaches 65°F, planting seeds 1 inch deep. Excellent for succession planting every 2-3 weeks.
color: Cream to golden-yellow exterior, pale yellow flesh
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild, and creamy with tender texture
culinary_uses: Individual serving portions, steaming whole, roasting, stuffing, side dishes, and decorative fall displays

common_pests: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, aphids

name: Yellow Crookneck Squash Early Summer
description: An improved selection of the classic yellow crookneck that produces earlier harvests and more uniform fruits than traditional varieties. This prolific summer squash delivers the beloved buttery flavor and tender texture of heirloom crooknecks while offering better disease resistance and more consistent production. The distinctive curved neck and bumpy skin make it instantly recognizable and highly sought after by gardeners who appreciate traditional American vegetables.

sowing_method: Direct sow after soil reaches 65°F, planting seeds 1 inch deep. Can succession plant every 2 weeks for continuous harvest.

common_pests: Squash bugs, cucumber beetles, squash vine borers
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, bacterial wilt, downy mildew

name: Cosmic Purple Carrot
description: A stunning hybrid carrot that produces 7-inch roots with deep purple skin and bright orange cores that create beautiful rings when sliced. This All-America Selections winner combines exceptional sweetness with eye-catching color, making it perfect for both fresh eating and adding visual drama to dishes. The unique bicolor appearance and crisp texture make it a favorite among gardeners looking to grow something truly special.

soil_type: Deep, loose, well-drained sandy loam free of rocks
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows 12 inches apart. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks.
color: Deep purple exterior with bright orange core
flavor_profile: Sweet and crisp with mild spicy notes from the purple skin
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, roasting, stir-fries, pickling. Excellent for adding color to dishes.

common_pests: Carrot fly, aphids, flea beetles, wireworms
common_diseases: Alternaria leaf blight, bacterial soft rot, aster yellows

name: Easter Egg Radish
description: Easter Egg Radish is a vibrant heirloom variety producing multicolored roots with a stunning mix of white, pink, purple, and red striping. These small, round radishes mature in approximately 30 days, making them ideal for quick succession planting. Their defining characteristic is their mild, crisp, and exceptionally sweet flavor with significantly less peppery bite than traditional radishes, making them particularly appealing to those who prefer gentler radish varieties. The colorful appearance and pleasant taste make them both visually striking in gardens and excellent for fresh eating, salads, and garnishes.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost. Plant seeds 1/2 inch deep, thin to proper spacing. Succession plant every 10 days.
color: Mixed colors: purple, pink, red, white
flavor_profile: Mild, crisp, and sweet with less bite than traditional radishes
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, garnishes, pickling, roasting when larger

common_pests: Flea beetles, root maggots, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew

name: All Seasons White Daikon
scientific_name: Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus 'All Seasons White'
description: A versatile Japanese daikon radish that can be grown in both spring and fall, producing impressive 14-18 inch long white roots weighing up to 2 pounds each. This reliable variety offers the classic mild, crisp texture that makes daikon so popular in Asian cuisine, while being much easier to grow than many specialty varieties. Perfect for gardeners wanting to explore Asian vegetables or those seeking a substantial, storage-friendly root crop.
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, deep consistent watering
soil_type: Deep, loose, well-drained soil free of obstacles

common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, bacterial soft rot

name: Nero Tondo Black Spanish Round Radish
description: An eye-catching heirloom winter radish from Spain that produces striking black-skinned roots with pure white flesh and a surprisingly mild flavor. This unique variety stores exceptionally well and actually improves in flavor after a few frosts, making it perfect for extending the harvest season into winter. The dramatic color contrast when sliced creates stunning visual appeal in dishes while delivering the crisp, clean taste that makes winter radishes so prized.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow in mid to late summer for fall harvest. Plant seeds 1/2 inch deep, thin when seedlings are 2 inches tall.

flavor_profile: Mild and sweet with crisp texture, less pungent than typical winter radishes
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, roasting, braising, pickling, winter storage

common_pests: Flea beetles, root maggots, cabbage worms

name: Mokum Carrot
description: Top-rated for flavor among our early varieties. Attractive slender roots are great for early bunches. A bit earlier than Yaya, more slender, the same length (5 1/2-6 1/2") and color, and with similar high sugar and brittle tenderness. Holds sweet taste even in warm weather. Short tops. Widely adapted. Amsterdam type. Also available with NOP-compliant pelleting.

soil_type: Deep, loose, well-drained sandy loam
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep, succession plant every 2-3 weeks through summer.

culinary_uses: Fresh eating, juicing, salads, roasting, steaming. Premium quality for all uses.

common_pests: Carrot fly, aphids, flea beetles, wireworms
common_diseases: Alternaria leaf blight, cercospora leaf spot, bacterial soft rot

name: Atomic Red Carrot
description: A stunning hybrid carrot that delivers both visual impact and exceptional flavor with its brilliant red-orange exterior and bright orange interior. These smooth, cylindrical roots grow uniformly and maintain their vibrant color when cooked, making them perfect for both fresh eating and culinary presentations. Gardeners love this variety for its reliable performance and the wow factor it brings to harvest baskets.

common_diseases: Alternaria leaf blight, bacterial soft rot, cavity spot

name: Cherry Belle Radish
description: America's most popular radish variety and an All-America Selections winner, Cherry Belle delivers reliable results for gardeners of all skill levels. These perfectly round, cherry-red radishes mature in just 22-30 days, making them ideal for quick satisfaction and succession planting throughout the growing season. Their mild, crisp bite and uniform shape make them a favorite for fresh eating and beautiful garnishes.

name: Yukon Gold Potato
description: Developed in Canada, Yukon Gold has become the gold standard for home gardeners seeking a versatile, all-purpose potato with exceptional flavor. The smooth, golden skin and creamy yellow flesh make these potatoes as beautiful as they are delicious, while their waxy-starchy texture works perfectly for everything from mashing to roasting. This reliable variety produces good yields and stores well, making it a cornerstone of the home garden.

Growing 8-10 inches long with distinctive black skin and pure white interior flesh, these storage radishes develop complex, pungent flavors that mellow beautifully when cooked. Perfect for gardeners seeking unusual varieties that provide fresh eating well into winter months.

The 7-8 inch roots develop beautiful orange color throughout and maintain their tender texture even when fully mature. Garden families especially appreciate this variety's consistent performance and kid-friendly sweet flavor that makes fresh vegetables irresistible.

Winner of the All-America Selections award for its exceptional performance and unique appeal to gardeners with space constraints.

Unlike typical small radishes, German Giant becomes milder and sweeter as it grows larger, making it perfect for cooking applications. German Butterball is considered one of the best-tasting potatoes available to home gardeners.

Unlike many turnips, Scarlet Queen delivers a distinctly sweet, mild flavor without bitter notes, making it excellent for both raw consumption in salads and cooked preparations. The vibrant skin color provides visual appeal while the tender texture remains consistent even when harvested at larger sizes. An easy-to-grow heirloom suitable for gardeners of all skill levels.

soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct seed in early spring 2-3 weeks before last frost or in late summer for fall harvest. Thin seedlings to proper spacing.

flavor_profile: Sweet, mild turnip flavor without bitterness, crisp texture

This reliable variety produces excellent yields of smooth, white-skinned potatoes with outstanding storage qualities. name: Black Spanish Round Radish

With its unique appearance and intense flavor, it's a conversation starter that brings old-world character to modern gardens.

name: Ailsa Craig Onion

Jumbo to colossal, round to teardrop-shaped bulbs with light yellow skins. Use fresh or for short-term storage. Adaptation: 38-60° latitude. Also available with NOP-compliant pelleting.

name: Touchon Carrot

This variety produces perfectly straight, smooth roots with little to no core, making every bite tender and flavorful. This traditional variety produces medium-sized blue-green heads with excellent flavor and reliable side shoot production after the main head is harvested. A must-grow for gardeners seeking authentic Italian flavor and continuous harvests.

name: Dwarf Blue Curled Kale
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. acephala 'Dwarf Blue Curled'
description: A compact heirloom kale variety dating back to the 1860s, prized for its intensely curled blue-green leaves and sweet flavor that improves with frost. This space-saving variety is perfect for small gardens and containers while still providing abundant harvests of tender, flavorful leaves. The attractive, heavily ruffled foliage also makes it an excellent ornamental edible.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in late summer for fall harvest. Transplant 2-4 weeks before last frost.
color: Blue-green with purple tinges in cold weather
shape: Heavily curled and ruffled leaves, compact rosette
flavor_profile: Sweet and mild, becoming sweeter after frost exposure
culinary_uses: Salads (young leaves), sautéing, soups, smoothies, chips, braising
disease_resistance: Good cold tolerance, moderate disease resistance
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage loopers
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew

name: Tokyo Bekana
description: This one-of-a-kind blonde leaf with ruffled texture adds loft and a pop of color to salad mixes. A non-heading Chinese cabbage that has buttery leaves with a crunchy rib and mild flavor. At full size, softly curled leaves on narrow, white petioles can be sold as bunched greens. Thin to 4-8" for full-size heads for bunching. Also available in organic seed.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good moisture retention
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring or late summer. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks. Can start indoors but direct sowing preferred.
flavor_profile: Very mild, sweet, and tender with no bitterness
culinary_uses: Stir-fries, salads, soups, steaming, pickling, raw snacking
disease_resistance: Moderate resistance to bolting in cool weather
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms
common_diseases: Clubroot, downy mildew, bacterial soft rot

name: Dinosaur Kale
description: Dinosaur Kale is a heirloom Brassica featuring distinctive bumpy, deeply textured dark green leaves that resemble dinosaur skin—the source of its charming name. Maturing in 125 days under full sun, this variety stands out for its remarkably sweet, nutty flavor with significantly less bitterness than curly kale varieties, making it exceptionally palatable both raw and cooked. The tender leaves are ideal for salads, smoothies, and sautéed dishes. Thriving in rich, well-drained soil amended with organic matter, Dinosaur Kale is an easy-to-grow variety perfect for both beginners and experienced gardeners.
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Direct seed 2-4 weeks before last spring frost or start indoors 4-6 weeks before transplanting. For fall harvest, sow 12-14 weeks before first fall frost.
color: Dark blue-green with prominent white veins
shape: Long, narrow, strap-like leaves with heavily textured surface
flavor_profile: Sweet, nutty flavor with less bitterness than curly kale varieties
culinary_uses: Excellent for sautéing, braising, soups, salads (massage raw leaves), smoothies, and traditional Italian dishes
disease_resistance: Good resistance to bolting and frost damage. Moderate resistance to common brassica diseases.
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage loopers
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew, white rust

name: Copenhagen Market Cabbage
description: A classic early-maturing cabbage that's been a gardener favorite since 1909, prized for its reliability and compact growth habit. This Danish heirloom produces perfectly round, solid heads with crisp, sweet leaves that are ideal for fresh eating or quick cooking. Its ability to mature quickly while maintaining excellent quality makes it perfect for successive plantings.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture especially during head formation
soil_type: Rich, fertile, well-drained soil with high organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last spring frost. Transplant when 4-6 inches tall. For fall crop, direct seed or transplant 12-14 weeks before hard frost.
color: Light to medium green outer leaves with pale green interior
flavor_profile: Crisp texture with sweet, mild cabbage flavor
culinary_uses: Excellent for coleslaw, salads, stir-fries, stuffed cabbage, and light cooking. Good fresh storage variety.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to splitting and bolting. Moderate disease resistance typical of heirlooms.
common_pests: Cabbage worms, cutworms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage root maggots
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, fusarium yellows, downy mildew

name: Waltham 29 Broccoli
description: Developed at the University of Massachusetts, this cold-hardy broccoli variety was specifically bred to extend the growing season well into winter in northern climates. It produces medium-sized, deep blue-green heads followed by an abundance of tender side shoots that can be harvested for weeks. This reliable performer is perfect for gardeners wanting fresh broccoli through the fall and early winter months.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture especially during head development
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last spring frost for spring crop. For fall harvest, start seeds indoors in mid-summer, 12-14 weeks before hard frost.
color: Deep blue-green heads with tight, compact florets
shape: Compact, dome-shaped central head with numerous side shoots
flavor_profile: Sweet, nutty flavor with excellent texture and minimal bitterness
culinary_uses: Excellent for steaming, roasting, stir-frying, and raw in salads. Both main heads and side shoots are delicious.
disease_resistance: Good cold tolerance and resistance to bolting. Moderate resistance to common broccoli diseases.
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage loopers
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew, alternaria leaf spot

name: Mizuna
description: Mizuna is a Japanese mustard green featuring delicate, feathery leaves with a lacy appearance that resemble fine parsley. This heirloom brassica matures quickly in 21-45 days, making it ideal for succession planting. Unlike assertive mustard greens, mizuna offers a mild, slightly peppery flavor with a fresh, clean taste that adds subtle complexity to salads and Asian dishes. The tender leaves are less fibrous than standard mustard varieties, allowing them to be eaten raw or lightly cooked. Mizuna thrives in cool seasons and tolerates partial shade, making it versatile for various growing conditions.
plant_spacing: 6-12 inches (closer for baby greens, wider for full-size)
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-8 hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct seed every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest. Sow ¼ inch deep. Can be grown year-round in mild climates, spring and fall in colder areas.
color: Bright green with deeply cut, feathery foliage
size: Individual leaves 3-8 inches long depending on harvest stage
shape: Deeply serrated, feathery leaves forming loose rosettes
flavor_profile: Mild, slightly peppery with a fresh, clean taste - much milder than most mustard greens
culinary_uses: Excellent in salad mixes, stir-fries, soups, and as a garnish. Baby leaves perfect raw, mature leaves good cooked.
disease_resistance: Good bolt resistance in cool weather. Generally pest and disease resistant due to quick growth.
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms (less susceptible than other brassicas)
common_diseases: Rarely affected due to quick growing cycle. Occasional downy mildew in humid conditions.

name: Burpee Hybrid Broccoli
description: A reliable hybrid broccoli that produces large, tight heads with excellent uniformity and heat tolerance. This variety is prized for its ability to produce substantial side shoots after the main head is harvested, extending the harvest season significantly.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with high organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. For fall harvest, start 12-14 weeks before first fall frost.
shape: Large, tight, dome-shaped heads with fine beads
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet broccoli flavor with tender stems
culinary_uses: Steaming, stir-frying, roasting, raw in salads, freezing
disease_resistance: Good resistance to downy mildew and black rot
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage loopers
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew

name: Winterbor Kale
description: Plants are tall, with excellent yield and good cold hardiness. The ruffled blue-green leaves have an attractive curl. Vigorous, productive plants.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow 10-12 weeks before first fall frost, or start indoors and transplant. Can also be sown in early spring 4-6 weeks before last frost.
color: Blue-green with purple tinting in cold weather
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild kale flavor, especially sweet after frost
culinary_uses: Salads, smoothies, sautéing, soups, kale chips, braising
disease_resistance: Excellent cold tolerance, resistant to bolting
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles
common_diseases: Black rot, clubroot, downy mildew

name: Early Flat Dutch Cabbage
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata 'Early Flat Dutch'
description: A classic heirloom cabbage dating back to the 1840s, prized for its large, flattened heads that can weigh 10-15 pounds. This reliable variety produces solid, sweet heads with excellent storage qualities and is perfect for making sauerkraut or coleslaw.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture important
soil_type: Rich, well-drained, fertile soil with high organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last spring frost for summer harvest, or 12-14 weeks before first fall frost for fall harvest.
color: Light to medium green outer leaves, pale green interior
flavor_profile: Sweet, mild cabbage flavor with tender texture
culinary_uses: Coleslaw, sauerkraut, stuffed cabbage, soups, braising, fresh eating
disease_resistance: Moderate disease resistance, good storage qualities
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage root maggots
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, fusarium yellows

name: Purple Vienna Kohlrabi
description: An eye-catching heirloom kohlrabi with striking purple-red skin and crisp white interior that tastes like a cross between cabbage and turnip. This unique vegetable is perfect for gardeners wanting to try something different, and it's surprisingly easy to grow with a sweet, mild flavor when harvested young.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow 4-6 weeks before last spring frost, or 8-10 weeks before first fall frost. Can succession plant every 2-3 weeks.
color: Purple-red skin with bright white interior
shape: Round, bulbous stem with leaves emerging from top
flavor_profile: Crisp, sweet, mild flavor similar to broccoli stem or mild turnip
culinary_uses: Raw in salads, roasting, stir-frying, steaming, pickling, coleslaw substitute
disease_resistance: Generally disease resistant, good bolt tolerance
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage worms, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew

name: Champion Collards
description: A heat and cold tolerant hybrid collard that produces large, smooth, blue-green leaves perfect for traditional Southern cooking. This variety is exceptionally slow to bolt and continues producing tender leaves through both summer heat and winter cold, making it ideal for year-round harvests in mild climates.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil, tolerates poor soils
sowing_method: Direct sow 4-6 weeks before last spring frost or 10-12 weeks before first fall frost. Can also start indoors and transplant.
shape: Large, broad, oval leaves with thick stems
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet flavor, less bitter than many brassicas
culinary_uses: Braising, steaming, sautéing, soups, traditional Southern dishes, wraps
disease_resistance: Good heat and cold tolerance, slow to bolt
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, harlequin bugs
common_diseases: Black rot, clubroot, downy mildew

name: Contender Green Beans
description: A reliable bush bean that's perfect for beginners and experienced gardeners alike. This hardy variety produces abundant yields of tender, stringless pods even in cool weather conditions. Contender's compact growth habit and disease resistance make it an excellent choice for home gardens with limited space.
water_requirement: 1 inch per week, avoid overhead watering
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow after soil temperature reaches 60°F and all danger of frost has passed. Plant 1-2 inches deep.
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender, and crisp with mild bean flavor
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, steaming, stir-frying, freezing, canning, casseroles
disease_resistance: Good resistance to common bean mosaic virus and powdery mildew
common_pests: Bean beetles, aphids, spider mites, thrips
common_diseases: Bacterial blight, rust, anthracnose, white mold

name: Bok Choy 'Joi Choi'
description: An All-America Selections winner that produces beautiful, uniform heads with crisp white stems and dark green leaves. This hybrid variety is slow to bolt and performs exceptionally well in both spring and fall gardens. Joi Choi's tender texture and mild flavor make it perfect for both raw and cooked applications.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-8 hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil high in organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow or transplant. For spring crop, sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. For fall crop, sow 10-12 weeks before first frost.

shape: Upright rosette with thick, spoon-shaped leaves
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet cabbage flavor with crisp, juicy stems
culinary_uses: Stir-fries, soups, salads, steaming, braising, kimchi, fresh eating
disease_resistance: Good resistance to bolting and tip burn
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms, diamondback moth
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew, white spot

name: Red Giant Mustard
description: A stunning Asian green with large, bronze-red leaves that add dramatic color to both the garden and the dinner plate. This vigorous heirloom variety provides continuous harvests of spicy, peppery leaves that mellow when cooked. Red Giant's bold appearance and robust flavor make it a favorite among adventurous gardeners and chefs.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring or late summer. Plant seeds 1/4 inch deep. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest.

shape: Large, broad, serrated leaves with prominent veining
flavor_profile: Spicy, peppery, mustard bite when raw; milder and sweeter when cooked
culinary_uses: Stir-fries, salads (young leaves), braising, steaming, pickling, microgreens

common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms, harlequin bugs
common_diseases: Downy mildew, white rust, black rot, clubroot

name: Cabbage 'Stonehead'
description: An award-winning hybrid that produces perfectly round, compact heads with exceptional storage quality. This reliable variety forms dense, 3-4 pound heads that resist splitting and maintain their quality in the garden longer than most cabbages. Stonehead's consistent performance and excellent flavor make it a top choice for home gardeners seeking reliability.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost for spring crop. Direct sow or transplant for fall crop 12-14 weeks before first hard frost.

flavor_profile: Sweet, mild cabbage flavor with crisp, tender texture
culinary_uses: Coleslaw, sauerkraut, stir-fries, soups, steaming, raw salads, storage

common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cutworms, cabbage root maggot
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, fusarium yellows, downy mildew

name: Tatsoi
description: Tatsoi is a compact Asian brassica featuring glossy, dark green spoon-shaped leaves arranged in a dense rosette, typically reaching 4-6 inches tall. Ready to harvest in 40-50 days, this heirloom variety offers a mild mustard flavor balanced with subtle sweetness and tender, succulent texture. Its small, self-contained growth habit makes it ideal for succession planting and container cultivation. Tatsoi works exceptionally well in stir-fries, salads, and Asian cuisine, where its delicate leaves and stems can be used whole.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-8 hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil rich in organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring or late summer. Plant seeds 1/4 inch deep. Can succession plant every 2-3 weeks.
size: Individual leaves 2-4 inches, rosette 8-10 inches across
shape: Flat rosette with round, spoon-shaped leaves
flavor_profile: Mild mustard flavor with slight sweetness, tender and succulent
culinary_uses: Stir-fries, salads, soups, steaming, braising, baby leaf salads, microgreens
disease_resistance: Very cold hardy, tolerates frost well, generally disease resistant
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms, leaf miners
common_diseases: Downy mildew, white spot, clubroot, black rot

name: Green Goliath Broccoli
description: A hybrid powerhouse that produces massive 6-8 inch heads on sturdy plants that tower over other broccoli varieties. Green Goliath lives up to its name with exceptional heat tolerance and the ability to produce substantial side shoots after the main head is harvested, extending your harvest season significantly.

soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. For fall crop, start 12-14 weeks before first fall frost.

flavor_profile: Sweet, mild, and tender with no bitterness
culinary_uses: Steaming, stir-frying, roasting, fresh eating, freezing well for later use

name: Red Acre Cabbage
description: An early-maturing red cabbage that forms compact, baseball-sized heads perfect for small families and succession planting. This reliable variety produces gorgeous deep purple-red heads that hold their color beautifully when cooked and adds stunning visual appeal to any garden or plate.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic content

disease_resistance: Good resistance to splitting and yellows

name: Vates Dwarf Blue Curled Kale
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. acephala 'Vates Dwarf Blue Curled'
description: A compact, cold-hardy kale variety that was specifically bred for both home gardens and commercial production, winning an AAS award for its reliability. The tightly curled, blue-green leaves pack incredible nutrition into a space-saving plant that actually improves in flavor after frost touches the leaves.

soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poor soils better than most brassicas

flavor_profile: Mild, sweet flavor that becomes sweeter after frost exposure

name: Osaka Purple Mustard
description: A stunning Japanese mustard green with deep purple leaves and bright green stems that creates an eye-catching contrast in any garden bed or salad bowl. This fast-growing beauty offers a mild mustard flavor when young that intensifies as leaves mature, giving you versatility from baby greens to cooking greens.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks. For fall crop, sow 8-10 weeks before first fall frost.
color: Deep purple leaves with bright green stems and veins
shape: Broad, flat leaves with scalloped edges
flavor_profile: Mild mustard flavor when young, becoming more peppery and pungent as leaves mature
culinary_uses: Baby greens for salads, microgreens, stir-frying, braising, adding color to mixed greens
disease_resistance: Good bolt resistance in cool weather
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms

name: Purple Sprouting Broccoli 'Early Purple Sprouting'
scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. italica 'Early Purple Sprouting'
description: A stunning heirloom variety that produces abundant purple flower heads over an extended harvest period. Unlike regular broccoli, this cold-hardy gem overwinters and provides fresh spears when little else is growing in the garden. The purple heads turn green when cooked and offer a sweet, nutty flavor that's considered superior to regular broccoli.

soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with plenty of organic matter
sowing_method: Start seeds indoors in late spring or direct sow in early summer for overwintering harvest. Plant out in midsummer.

size: Small to medium heads, 2-4 inches across
shape: Loose, sprouting clusters rather than tight heads
flavor_profile: Sweet, nutty, and tender with less bitterness than regular broccoli
culinary_uses: Steam, stir-fry, roast, or eat raw. Excellent in pasta dishes and grain bowls. Entire stem and leaves are edible.

common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage root fly
common_diseases: Clubroot, downy mildew, black rot, white rust

name: Cabbage 'Red Express'
description: A compact, early-maturing hybrid that produces perfectly round, deep red-purple heads in just 76 days. This space-saving variety is ideal for small gardens and containers while delivering the crisp texture and peppery-sweet flavor that makes red cabbage a kitchen favorite. The uniform heads resist splitting and store exceptionally well.

soil_type: Rich, well-drained soil with good organic content
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost date. Can succession plant for fall harvest.

culinary_uses: Perfect for coleslaw, braising, pickling, and fermentation. Adds color to stir-fries and salads. Excellent for sauerkraut.
disease_resistance: Good resistance to fusarium yellows and black rot
common_pests: Cabbage worms, cutworms, aphids, flea beetles
common_diseases: Clubroot, black leg, downy mildew, white rust

description: The ultimate winter kale that actually improves in flavor after frost exposure, becoming sweeter and more tender. This vigorous hybrid produces masses of deeply curled, blue-green leaves that can withstand temperatures down to 10°F while continuing to grow. It's the go-to variety for gardeners wanting fresh greens throughout winter.

name: Radish 'Daikon 'Miyashige'
description: A premium Japanese daikon variety prized for its exceptionally sweet, crisp flesh and smooth white skin. These impressively large roots can reach 14-18 inches long while maintaining perfect tenderness throughout. This variety is particularly valued for its ability to stay mild and juicy even when large, making it perfect for fresh eating and traditional Asian preparations.

water_requirement: 1-1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture for best texture
soil_type: Deep, loose, well-drained soil free of stones and debris

name: Mustard Greens 'Southern Giant Curled'
description: A beloved heirloom variety that's been a Southern garden staple for over 100 years, producing massive yields of deeply frilled, bright green leaves. This heat-tolerant variety grows vigorously in conditions that would stress other greens, making it perfect for extending the harvest season. The young leaves are mild enough for salads, while mature leaves develop the characteristic peppery bite that makes them perfect for traditional Southern cooking.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poorer soils than most brassicas
sowing_method: Direct sow from early spring through fall. Can succession plant every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest.
color: Bright green with lighter green veins
size: Large leaves, 8-12 inches long when mature
shape: Deeply curled and frilled edges, broad leaves
flavor_profile: Young leaves are mild and slightly sweet. Mature leaves have a spicy, peppery mustard flavor with slight bitterness.
culinary_uses: Young leaves excellent in salads and sandwiches. Mature leaves perfect for braising, sautéing, and traditional Southern preparations with ham or bacon.
disease_resistance: Good heat tolerance, moderate resistance to downy mildew and white rust
common_pests: Flea beetles, aphids, cabbage worms, harlequin bugs
common_diseases: White rust, downy mildew, black rot, clubroot

name: Cress Microgreens
description: Also known as "Curly Cress" or "Peppergrass." Try adding cress to a sandwich or when an extra-spicy bite is needed. Based on our yield trial, we recommend seeding 10.5 gm per tray at approx. 3 flats per oz of seed. Avg. days to maturity was 13 days when harvested at the first true leaf (as opposed to cotyledon) stage. Also available in organic seed.
sun_requirement: Indirect light or grow lights 12-16 hours daily
water_requirement: Mist 2-3 times daily, keep growing medium moist
soil_type: Soilless growing medium, coconut coir, or hydroponic mats
sowing_method: Scatter seeds densely on pre-moistened growing medium. Cover lightly and mist daily.
color: Bright green leaves with white stems
shape: Small oval leaves on tender white stems
flavor_profile: Sharp, peppery, and pungent with a slight mustard-like heat
culinary_uses: Salads, sandwiches, soups, garnishes, egg dishes, and as a spicy accent to mild foods
disease_resistance: Generally disease-free due to quick harvest cycle
common_pests: Aphids (rare), fungus gnats
common_diseases: Damping off if overwatered

name: Spinach Microgreens
description: Spinach microgreens are tender, delicate seedlings with small oval leaves and a vibrant green color. They reach harvest maturity in approximately 25 days, making them a moderately quick crop compared to full-size spinach. The key differentiator is their concentrated nutrient density—microgreens pack significantly more vitamins and minerals per gram than mature leaves. They deliver a mild, clean flavor with subtle sweetness and no bitterness, making them ideal for raw applications like salads, smoothie bowls, and garnishes. Their tender texture and refined taste work well in both culinary and presentation-focused dishes.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Bottom watering preferred, keep soil moist but not soggy
soil_type: Fine potting mix or seed starting blend
sowing_method: Soak seeds 12-24 hours in cool water. Sow densely on pre-moistened growing medium and cover lightly.
color: Bright to dark green leaves with pale green stems
size: 2-3 inch tall with first true leaves beginning

description: Lettuce microgreens are tender seedlings harvested at 1-2 inches tall, typically ready within 7-10 days of sprouting. These delicate greens feature soft, pale green leaves with a mild, slightly sweet flavor and buttery texture—distinctly different from mature lettuce. Unlike full-sized lettuce, microgreens are consumed whole, including the stem, delivering concentrated nutrients in a compact form. They excel as salad toppings, sandwich garnishes, and plate decorations, adding visual appeal and gentle flavor without bitterness. Their quick growth cycle and minimal space requirements make them ideal for indoor cultivation and home gardening beginners.

description: Carrot Microgreens are delicate, feathery seedlings harvested at 16-25 days, showcasing fine, fern-like green foliage with pale root stems. This heirloom variety offers a distinctive sweet carrot taste balanced by earthy, slightly bitter herbal notes uncommon in typical microgreens. Best grown in fine, well-draining seed starting mix under full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours), they present a moderate to challenging cultivation difficulty but reward growers with vibrant, nutrient-dense greens perfect for garnishing and flavor complexity in dishes.

name: Red Clover Microgreens
description: A tall (2-3'), quick-growing clover. Sow in spring, summer, or fall, alone or with grain/grass. Will grow in more acidic soil (pH 5.0-6.0) than other clovers if lime is applied at seeding time. Widely grown biennial used for Nitrogen addition and hay crops. Large plant with big leaves makes it an ideal grazing crop. Red clover may be the best choice for frost seeding; it is extremely cold hardy and does well in most soils and growing conditions. it does, like most clovers, perform poorly in hot weather unless seeded into a crop canopy. Incorporate fully for best results. Mammoth Red clover will fix between 70-110 lb. nitrogen per acre. The long taproots loosen soils and mine phosphorus and other nutrients from deep in the soil. It should be seeded with 48-64 lb. or 1.5-2 bushels/acre of oats to "nurse" the clover. Inoculate with Alfalfa/True Clover Inoculant (#7535) for best performance. Edible Flowers: The flowers of red clover may be used to garnish drinks, salads, soups, and desserts; the flavor is sweet and mild.

description: Cabbage Microgreens are tender, delicate seedlings harvested at 7-14 days, well before the 68-day full plant maturity. These vibrant green microgreens feature a mild cabbage taste with a fresh, crisp texture and subtle peppery finish. They're prized for their nutrient density and visual appeal in salads, garnishes, and culinary presentations. Easy to grow indoors using fine potting mix or coconut coir, they require 4-6+ hours of light daily. Their key differentiator is rapid harvest time compared to full-sized cabbage, making them ideal for home gardeners and chefs seeking quick, fresh-from-seed produce with concentrated nutritional value.

name: Chives Microgreens
description: Chives microgreens are tender, slender green shoots with a mild onion flavor and fresh, aromatic profile. These delicate microgreens mature in 75-85 days and are ideal for culinary applications requiring a subtle allium taste without pungency. They thrive in fine seed starting mix or soilless medium under full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours daily). The heirloom variety offers a distinctly fresh character perfect for garnishing, salads, and dishes where gentle onion notes enhance rather than dominate. Moderate difficulty indoor growing requires vigilance against fungus gnats and thrips, making proper ventilation essential for success.

name: Buckwheat Microgreens
description: Buckwheat is widely grown as a grain crop, bee pasture, soil improving cover crop and as wildlife cover. It is a warm season grain which grows rapidly during the summer and several crops per year may be had with proper management. This rapid and dense growth chokes out weeds and is used in crop-free fields in rotation with vegetables. Buckwheat is often grown to attract beneficial insects and as pasture for honey bees. Sow in late May through July and till in about a month later, when flowering has begun. For grain harvest, sow 3 months before fall frost. Harvest after killing frost.

Ready to harvest in 50-60 days, these heirloom microgreens deliver an intense anise-licorice flavor that is remarkably aromatic and concentrated. The strong, distinctive taste makes them a specialty garnish for gourmet cuisines, particularly suited for seafood dishes, Mediterranean preparations, and culinary applications where bold licorice notes are desired. Grown indoors under full sun conditions with well-draining seed-starting mix, fennel microgreens require moderate cultivation skill and careful monitoring for aphids and fungus gnats.
