name: Iron Apricot
description: Early single-stem, or column-type stock in a highly uniform series for one-cut harvest. Aptly named for strong, straight, rigid stems. Iron performed well in our spring, early summer, and fall trials. Selectable for doubles and 55% double without selection. Compared to Katz, Iron is slightly later to flower, and has somewhat shorter and thicker stems. Tightly spaced florets on the flower spike make for compact 1 1/2-2", creamy peach-colored blooms with a full appearance. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil, neutral to slightly alkaline
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost

flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with subtle spice notes
culinary_uses: Garnish for salads, desserts, cocktails

name: Winged Everlasting
description: The prolific 1/2- 3/4" silvery-white blooms on stiff stems are typically grown for dried-flower production but are also useful as fresh cuts for flower arrangements. The tiny blooms make an elegant addition to boutonnieres and floral crowns. As flowers mature, the papery white petals open to reveal bright-yellow centers. Flowers are typically harvested when half open, before the center is exposed. Winged Everlasting gets its name from ribbed, or winged stems the plant produces. Tender perennial in Zones 9-10 but most often grown as an annual. Once the flowers were fully open at our trial farm, we noticed lots of flying friends visiting the flowers, including hoverflies and wasps.
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost, or start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost
shape: Small rounded blooms on stiff, ribbed stems

name: Bishop
description: For summer and fall crops. Bishop has well-wrapped heads, excellent vigor, and wide adaptability.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture (1-1.5 inches per week)
soil_type: Well-drained loam, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0-7.5)
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost and transplant, or direct sow after last frost
flavor_profile: Mild, tender, and sweet with minimal bitterness, ideal for fresh eating and cooked applications.

common_pests: Cabbage worms, flea beetles, cabbage loopers, diamondback moths

name: Sorrel
description: Sorrel microgreens are delicate, tender shoots harvested at 10 days with bright green cotyledons and thin stems. These heirloom varieties deliver an intense lemony flavor with pronounced acidic tartness, making them ideal for elevating salads, soups, and gourmet plating. Quick-growing and easy to cultivate, they add a distinctive bright, tangy note that distinguishes them from common microgreens.
flavor_profile: Intense lemony flavor with bright, acidic tartness.
culinary_uses: Salads, cocktails, soups, sauces, fish dishes

name: KN-Bravo
description: KN-Bravo's internal color ranges from pale purple to white with purple streaks. Lends beautiful color to kimchi or fresh salads. Roots average 4-6" x 2 1/2-3" with good, sweet eating quality. Suitable for spring and summer sowings. NOTE: This variety contains 5-10% white-rooted off types.
water_requirement: Regular; 1-1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture essential
soil_type: Well-draining, loose loam; friable texture essential for straight root development
sowing_method: Direct sow ¼ inch deep, 2-3 weeks before last spring frost, or mid-to-late summer for fall crops; thin seedlings to 2-3 inches apart
color: Pale purple to white with purple streaks (interior); dark green foliage (greens)
shape: Cylindrical to slightly oblong root, 4-6 inches long and 2.5-3 inches diameter
flavor_profile: Sweet, crisp, mild radish flavor with tender texture and minimal pungency; pleasant crunch in fresh preparations
culinary_uses: Fresh salads, vegetable platters, salsas, kimchi fermentation, quick pickling, Asian cuisine garnish
common_pests: Flea beetles, root maggots (less common in hybrid radishes), cabbage moths
common_diseases: Fusarium wilt (rare in well-drained soil), bacterial leaf spot (minor in cool seasons)

name: Carlton
description: Compact plant with good heat and cold tolerance. Better uniformity and yield than open-pollinated varieties. Traditionally used in Japan either steamed and seasoned with soy sauce, or in stir fries and soups. It is also excellent braised or, at the baby leaf stage, used in salads.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic to neutral pH
sowing_method: Direct sow or transplant seedlings started indoors 3-4 weeks before planting. Can sow every 2 weeks for continuous harvest.
flavor_profile: Mild, tender, and crisp with a subtle sweetness; versatile for both raw and cooked applications.
culinary_uses: Salads, steamed with soy sauce, stir-fries, soups, braising, baby leaf stage salads
common_pests: Aphids, slugs, snails, flea beetles
common_diseases: Downy mildew, lettuce mosaic virus, tipburn

name: Elan
description: Elan has a vigorous habit (many runners) and white flowers that produce medium-size, conical berries with intensely sweet flavor. Suitable for field, greenhouse, and container production. Produces fruit the first year and bears from June through frost. Grows best in Zones 5-8.Edible Flowers: Flowers add a mild, strawberry-like flavor if sprinkled on salads or when used as a garnish for drinks and desserts.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic
sowing_method: Transplant in spring after last frost
flavor_profile: Intensely sweet with classic strawberry flavor, producing edible white flowers with mild strawberry-like taste.
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, desserts, jams, preserves, edible flower garnishes
common_pests: Spider mites, slugs, aphids
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, leaf spot, gray mold

name: Mahogany Splendor
description: Decorative, deeply cut foliage with maple-leaf appearance, rich color, and sturdy branches. Ideal for late summer and fall arrangements. Primarily grown for use as cut flower foliage, the vigorous, drought- and heat-tolerant plants are also suitable for containers and landscaping. The abundant color and tall stature make Mahogany Splendor a lovely backdrop in the garden or patio. NOTE: If grown indoors or in part shade, foliage will be green to rust in color; the mahogany color develops fully in full sun. Also known as red-leaf hibiscus, false roselle, African rose mallow, and cranberry hibiscus. Tropical, herbaceous perennial in Zones 8a-9b. Tender perennial or annual in all other growing zones.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil; slightly acidic to neutral pH preferred
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost in zones 8a-9b

name: Elegant Salmon
description: Beautiful salmon blooms strung along purple-red stems. Flowers bloom from the lower portion of the stem and continue to open up the stem. Mostly double, semidouble, and single blooms. Easy to harvest, as the hard (almost woody) stems are nearly free of foliage. Basal-branching plants. Native to the Western US. Also known as elegant clarkia and mountain garland.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost
shape: Double, semidouble, and single blooms strung along stems

name: Green 70 D Improved
description: Very similar to Gunsho, just a few days later to mature. Choi sums are among the most popular vegetables in Hong Kong, Taiwan, and China. The stems are a vibrant bright green, very sweet and tender, and are usually harvested just before, or as the first few buds are opening. The stems regrow for cut-and-come-again harvest. Excellent in stir-fries, steamed, or in soups.
soil_type: Well-draining loam, moderately fertile
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks before planting out
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender stems with a mild, delicate flavor and crisp texture.
culinary_uses: Stir-fries, steamed dishes, soups

name: Comanche
description: Beautiful shanks can achieve impressive length. Comanche is a midseason leek with medium blue-green foliage. Its upright plant habit makes for easier cultivation. Also available in organic seed.
plant_spacing: 4-6 inches between plants, 18-24 inches between rows
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained loam, rich in organic matter, slightly acidic to neutral
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last spring frost, or direct sow after last frost
shape: Long cylindrical shanks with upright foliage
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet flavor with delicate, slightly grassy undertones and tender texture
culinary_uses: Soups, stews, braised dishes, grilled preparations, sautéing, creamy sauces
common_pests: Leek moths, onion flies, thrips, root maggots
common_diseases: Pink root, white rot, fusarium basal rot, downy mildew

name: Cheap Frills Mix
description: A colorful kaleidoscope of highly textured leaves that provides visual interest and excellent loft. An exceptional combination of textures, flavors, and colors. Includes red and green mustard, mizuna, and tatsoi. Varieties are subject to change depending upon availability.
soil_type: Well-draining loam with organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow outdoors in cool seasons
flavor_profile: A peppery, tangy, and delicate blend combining peppery mustard notes with subtle mizuna complexity and mild tatsoi undertones.
culinary_uses: Salads, garnishes, fresh eating, mixed greens, light sautéing
common_pests: Aphids, flea beetles, slugs
common_diseases: Lettuce mosaic virus, downy mildew, powdery mildew

name: Bloody Mary
description: Dramatic 2" flowers in shades of dark red, coral red, cream, and unique bicolors. This mix is generally compact, but contains both mounding and trailing types. Foliage ranges from dark blue-green to medium-green. 2019 Fleuroselect Novelty winner.
water_requirement: Regular, keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged
soil_type: Well-drained potting soil or garden soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after last frost
color: Dark red, coral red, and cream with bicolors

name: Kalebration Kale Mix
description: A brilliant blend of rich greens and reds that shows deep color contrast in all growing slots. Striking the right balance of colors and textures, this mix can be marketed on its own or added to baby leaf lettuce. Strong regrowth allows for cut-and-come-again harvesting. Selected for baby-leaf production. Varieties are subject to change depending upon availability.

name: Avignon Silver II
description: Exceptionally robust stems, high yields, and uniform growth in the field or greenhouse when grown under short days and low temperatures. Group 2 snapdragon. Previously known as Costa Silver II. Edible Flowers: The flowers are a colorful garnish for use in salads, desserts, and drinks. The flavor is floral and slightly bitter, so use sparingly.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular; consistent moisture without waterlogging. Allow soil to dry slightly between waterings.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil; pH 6.0–7.0
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before target transplant date. Sow on surface of seed-starting mix (seeds require light to germinate). Keep moist at 65-70°F. Transplant outdoors after last frost or in mid-summer for fall flowering.
shape: Vertical flower spike with densely packed individual florets
flavor_profile: Delicate, distinctly floral with a subtle bitter finish; use sparingly as a garnish
culinary_uses: Edible garnish for salads, desserts, cocktails, and plated dishes
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, whiteflies, thrips
common_diseases: Snapdragon rust, powdery mildew, root rot (in waterlogged soil), damping-off (in cool, wet conditions)

name: Vintage Brown
description: Tightly spaced florets on thick, strong, straight stems. Single-stem/column-type stock for one-cut harvest. Vintage Brown is not brown but rose-taupe in color. This variety (updated in 2023) is the newest version of Vintage Brown and has a slightly warmer color palette and a touch of light gold at the flower center, as opposed to the original Vintage Brown, which was fully rose in color.Produces about 55% double-flowering plants. Overall, very similar to the Iron series in performance. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
shape: Tightly spaced florets on thick, straight stems
flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with subtle spice notes.
culinary_uses: Salad garnish, dessert topping, drink garnish, crystallized flowers

name: Antique Pink
description: Tightly spaced florets on thick, strong, straight stems. Single-stem or column type stock for one-cut harvest. Blooms have chartreuse green centers with pale pink edges. Plants produce about 55% double-flowering plants. Overall, very similar to the Iron series in performance. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
color: pale pink with chartreuse green centers
shape: tightly spaced florets on single columnar stem
flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with subtle spice notes.
culinary_uses: garnish for salads, desserts, drinks

name: Celway™ White
description: Celway® White is an heirloom ornamental flower variety that matures in 90-100 days under full sun conditions. It produces pristine white blooms and is prized for its clean, elegant appearance in gardens and floral arrangements. This easy-to-grow variety is valued primarily as a decorative flower rather than for culinary or medicinal purposes.
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost

name: Celway™ Terracotta
description: Celway™ Terracotta is a striking heirloom flower variety prized for its distinctive warm terracotta-colored blooms. Reaching maturity in 90-100 days, this easy-to-grow ornamental thrives in full sun and well-drained, fertile soil. The defining characteristic is its rich, warm peachy-orange hue that evokes traditional terracotta pottery, making it an excellent choice for cottage gardens, mixed borders, and cut flower arrangements. A reliable performer for gardeners of all skill levels.
water_requirement: Regular watering, keeping soil consistently moist but not waterlogged
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost date
shape: Flower spikes in 2-4 inch dense clusters

name: Coco™ Gold
description: Tall, sturdy plants produce an abundance of 2 1/2-3 1/2" fully double golden blooms for cut flowers and garden beds. The Coco™ series provides uniform flowering time and plant height across all three colors with excellent productivity for cut flower production. Flower color and flower size are comparable to Giant Yellow. Plants are slightly taller and later to bloom than Giant Yellow in our trials. Bold, uniform flowers are also useful for marigold garlands. Also known as African marigold, American marigold, and Aztec marigold. Edible Flowers: Use the flowers to dress up salads and desserts or cooked in egg or rice dishes. Flavor is floral with hints of citrus and spice, and slightly bitter. Remove the petals from the flower base before consuming as the base can be quite bitter.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Moderate; prefers drier conditions once established; drought-tolerant compared to many ornamentals
soil_type: Well-drained loam; tolerates poor soil but prefers fertile, slightly acidic conditions
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after all frost danger has passed
flavor_profile: Floral flavor with hints of citrus and spice; mild pleasant bitterness; edible petals only (remove bitter flower base)
culinary_uses: Salad garnish, dessert decoration, cooked in egg dishes, rice dishes, edible flower arrangements
common_pests: Spider mites (in hot, dry conditions), aphids, whiteflies
common_diseases: Powdery mildew (in humid conditions), root rot (in waterlogged soil)

name: Lemon Bush
description: Medium-green, 2-7", sword-shaped leaves that produce a strong citronella fragrance. Leaf and stem tips turn a lovely red color in late summer/fall. Less durable foliage than standard eucalyptus.
water_requirement: Regular; allow top inch of soil to dry between waterings
color: Medium green with red-tipped foliage in late summer/fall

name: Jumper
description: Very upright foliage keeps leaves off the ground, which results in easier cultivation, better plant health, and cleaner leaves at the wash station. Refined shanks with minimal bulbing for easy stripping.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
sowing_method: Transplant seedlings 4-6 inches apart in spring or late summer
flavor_profile: Mild, clean allium flavor with tender, refined texture.
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, garnishes, stir-fries, soups
disease_resistance: Common Rust (Intermediate); Purple Blotch (Intermediate); White Tip (Intermediate)

name: Song TJS-65
description: Green-stem cauliflower is popular in Asia, where it is commonly grown. Sweeter and more tender than standard white-stemmed varieties, and with longer stems that are exceptional for dipping. Kitchen prep work is quick, as a few knife strokes will cut most of the florets from the heads. Typically harvested after florets have elongated and curds have started to separate, though harvesting at the same stage as standard types is acceptable. Song TJS-65 performs better in hot weather and below-average fertility than standard types. Also known as sweet stem, soft curd, or loose curd cauliflower.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; 1-2 inches per week
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam with consistent moisture
sowing_method: Start indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost danger passes
shape: Elongated stems with separated, loose curds
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender, and mild with exceptional stem flavor superior to standard white cauliflower varieties.
culinary_uses: Raw dipping, stir-frying, steaming, roasting, blanching, Asian cuisine
common_pests: Cabbage worms, flea beetles, cabbage moths, aphids
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, downy mildew, leaf spot

name: ProCut® Horizon
description: ProCut® Horizon offers a highly uniform bloom time for a more concentrated harvest. Blooms are also perfectly cheerful, symmetrical, and open wide and flat with a similar appearance to Vincent®s Choice. Compared to ProCut Orange, ProCut Horizon has a more refined appearance, is more uniform, and has slightly smaller blooms (within 4-6" range) on taller stems. Pollenless. Single stem. Day-length neutral.
color: Golden-yellow (based on Horizon cultivar naming and comparison to Vincent's Choice)

name: ProCut® Orange DMR
description: Compared to standard ProCut® Orange, ProCut® Orange DMR is taller and slightly later to bloom. Otherwise, the flower color, form, and uniformity are very similar. NOTE: Downy mildew in sunflowers persists in infected soils and is especially problematic in cool, wet soils. Symptoms include damping-off, stunted plants, yellowing leaves, and the presence of downy, white mildew on the undersides of the leaves. Pollenless. Single stem.

name: King Richard
description: Beautiful full-sized leeks. In favorable soil and culture, the white shanks are over a foot long to the first leaf. Medium-green leaves with full habit. For baby leeks, plant closely (40 seeds/ft.) and harvest at finger size. While not hardy enough for overwintering, they will withstand medium-heavy frost (32° to 20°F/0° to -7°C) without losing their healthy appearance.
plant_spacing: 4-6 inches (standard), or 2-3 inches for baby leeks
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; keep soil evenly moist but not waterlogged
soil_type: Well-drained loam with rich organic matter, slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.0-7.0)
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date and transplant seedlings; or direct sow in mid-summer for fall/winter harvest
shape: Long, cylindrical white shanks with upright green foliage
flavor_profile: Mild, slightly sweet onion-like flavor with tender, delicate white shanks
culinary_uses: Soups, braising, sautéing, stir-fries, gratins, side dishes
common_pests: Leek moth, onion thrips, leek rust mite
common_diseases: Leek rust, fusarium wilt, pink root

name: Red King 2
description: Mild, crisp, and juicy into late maturity (like KN-Bravo, but sweeter). Remarkably uniform. Tall, upright tops with red and green petioles. A beauty on display, especially when paired with purple and white daikons. Root size adaptable to plant spacing. Can produce radishes over 1 lb., 5-8" long x 2-2 1/2" wide. Slow bolting and good field-holding ability. For both spring and summer sowings.
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; approximately 1 inch per week
soil_type: Loose, well-draining soil rich in organic matter, pH 6.0-7.0
sowing_method: Direct sow ¼ inch deep after soil warms to 50°F; suitable for spring and summer sowings
shape: Long cylindrical, 5-8 inches long, 2-2½ inches wide
flavor_profile: Mild, crisp, and juicy with subtle sweetness; less peppery than globe radishes, maintaining tender texture even at large sizes
culinary_uses: Fresh sliced in salads, vegetable platters, eaten with butter and salt, quick pickling, fermentation
common_pests: Flea beetles, cabbage root maggots, aphids
common_diseases: Bacterial leaf spot, fusarium wilt, powdery mildew

name: ProCut® Gold Lite DMR
description: Compared to standard ProCut® Gold, ProCut® Gold Lite DMR is taller, slightly later to bloom, and has wider, more rounded petals for a refined appearance. 4-6" blooms. Pollenless. Single stem. NOTE: Downy mildew in sunflowers persists in infected soils and is especially problematic in cool, wet soils. Symptoms include damping-off, stunted plants, yellowing leaves, and the presence of downy, white mildew on the undersides of the leaves.
soil_type: Well-drained loam
sowing_method: Direct sow outdoors after last frost when soil has warmed
common_pests: Sunflower beetles, aphids, cutworms
common_diseases: Downy mildew, Sclerotinia wilt, Phomopsis stem canker

name: Kelvin
description: Thick, juicy, and succulent. Petioles and leaves are a healthy dark green and stalks hold well in the field. Kelvin performs well under heat and moisture stress and in soils of average fertility. Upright and uniform. Also available with NOP-compliant pelleting.
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam; tolerates average fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost, transplant after last frost date
flavor_profile: Crisp, juicy, and tender with a classic mild celery flavor and clean taste.
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, salads, soups, stews, braising, snacking, cooking

name: Twister
description: The big, heavy, well-domed heads have large wrapper leaves that protect them from the sun, resulting in excellent color and head quality. Twister performs well in summer and fall in the Northeast and Midwest, from late June through late October in Coastal California, and in desert areas in the winter. A great complement to Mardi.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular; 1-1.5 inches per week; consistent moisture essential for quality head development
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam enriched with compost or aged manure; slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.0-7.0)

Florets are densely packed on the flower spike, giving the blooms a lovely full appearance. Bred for performance under short days and low temperatures; considered a group 2 snapdragon. Well-suited for an early spring bloom period. Performs well in our overwinter tunnel trials - a great color for early spring bouquets. Edible Flowers: The flowers are a colorful garnish for use in salads, desserts, and drinks. The flavor is floral and slightly bitter, so use sparingly.

Also known as pansy, Johnny jump-up, European field pansy, heart's ease, and hybrid violet. Edible Flowers: Decorative and edible garnish for salads and desserts with slight wintergreen flavor. flavor_profile: Mild, fresh, herbaceous parsley flavor with a clean, slightly grassy finish.
culinary_uses: Garnish, fresh chopping, salads, soups, sauces, herb blends
common_pests: Parsley worm, spider mites, aphids
common_diseases: Leaf spot, septoria, fusarium wilt

name: Clementine
description: Our brightest orange cauliflower. Big sturdy plants produce excellent crops, even under less-than-ideal conditions. The earliest maturing orange variety in our trials. For summer and fall harvest.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; 1-1.5 inches per week
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam, pH 6.0-7.5
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in summer for fall harvest
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet, tender orange cauliflower with a slightly buttery flavor and less sulfurous taste than white varieties.
culinary_uses: Roasting, steaming, raw in salads, stir-fries, curries, pureed soups
common_pests: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles, cabbage loopers
common_diseases: Black rot, clubroot, powdery mildew, downy mildew

name: Greater Quaking Grass
description: 1/2-1" long seedheads turn from light green to a beautiful, golden straw color when dried or left on the plants. Stems of these nodding, dancing beauties are a must-have for adding bits of sparkle and detail to design work. Productive plants produce abundant 12-20" long stems useful for fresh or dried cut flowers. The thin stems of this clump-forming grass are best suited for design work and not recommended for when something sturdy is needed. Beautiful addition to containers or garden beds. Might self-sow if seedheads are left on the plants. Tolerant to dry growing conditions once established. Also commonly known as big quaking grass, rattlesnake grass, and large quaking grass.
soil_type: Well-drained soil; tolerates dry conditions once established
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost once soil has warmed

name: Apricotta
description: Tall, vigorous, densely branching plants. Delicate single blooms have a slightly ruffled edge and glow in shades of blush, apricot, and rose. We have observed variation in bloom color: some flowers are lighter in color, while some express more rose than apricot. The 2 1/2-3" blooms are not as full or uniform as we would like. Still, we appreciate the variety for its productive plants and its ability to unify warm and cool hues in arrangements.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
shape: Single blooms with slightly ruffled edges

name: Grace Salmon
description: Each stem produces clusters of 1 1/2-3" wide, cup-shaped blooms. Sturdy stems and upward-facing bloom clusters work well as cut flowers. Bright, salmon-colored blooms add a cheerful and almost tropical vibe to mixed bouquets and arrangements. Supporting plants for straight stems is recommended. Also known as farewell-to-spring and satin flower.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Grace Shell Pink
description: Each stem produces clusters of 1 1/2-3" wide, cup-shaped blooms. Sturdy stems and upward-facing bloom clusters work well as cut flowers. White with blushy, shell-pink petals. Blooms add a cheerful and almost tropical vibe to mixed bouquets and arrangements. Supporting plants for straight stems is recommended. Also known as farewell-to-spring and satin flower.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Pink Pokers
description: Whimsical and wild statice looks to be a thing of fairytales or a sea-dwelling creature. Compared to L. sinuatum types, this statice has a very different look but is also earlier and easier to harvest. Plants have mostly basal leaves, which makes the stems easy to locate for cutting, and there are almost no leaves to strip away. Low-growing plants produced 2-3 flushes of harvestable stems in our trials. Excellent for dried flowers or fresh cuts. Flowers dry beautifully, holding their color well. Early statice. Also commonly known as Russian statice and rat tail statice.
water_requirement: Low to moderate; drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Lean, well-drained soil; tolerates sandy and slightly alkaline conditions
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; surface sow and press in (needs light to germinate). Transplant after last frost.
shape: Slender, curving tapered spikes resembling fuzzy tails
common_pests: Aphids, occasional thrips
common_diseases: Crown rot, root rot, botrytis (gray mold) in humid conditions

name: Madame Butterfly Cherry Bronze
description: Also known as an azalea type, Madame Butterfly's double petals create full, fluffy blooms for a Victorian look. The rose-coral/apricot color has a bit more of a rose hue compared to Madame Butterfly Bronze. For outdoor or greenhouse production. Bloom time comparable to group 3-4 "main season" or "all season" varieties. Edible Flowers: The flowers are a colorful garnish for use in salads, desserts, and drinks. The flavor is floral and slightly bitter, so use sparingly.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular; consistently moist but never waterlogged
soil_type: Well-draining, slightly acidic to neutral soil; azalea-formulated mix preferred
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last spring frost on soil surface (seeds require light). Transplant outdoors after all frost risk passes.
flavor_profile: Distinctly floral with subtle bitterness; delicate and refined, requiring minimal use to avoid overpowering dishes.
culinary_uses: Edible garnish for salads, desserts, and beverages; herbal tea infusions when dried
common_pests: Spider mites, azalea lace bugs, whiteflies
common_diseases: Botrytis (gray mold), azalea leaf gall, powdery mildew

name: Versailles Red
description: Blooms are 2 1/2" across and are a beautiful, dark burgundy/magenta red color. Strong, straight stems can withstand considerable handling. Produces under short days.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Savannah Grass
description: This late-season grass is the perfect color for late summer and fall arrangements, containers, and garden beds. 3-6" long, gracefully arched, rose gold to dusty rose plumes can be used as fresh or dried cut flowers. Panicles lighten in color when dried but maintain a rosy hue. 18-24" stems float above the compact, mounded grass clumps, making them easy to pluck from the plants. The thin stems are best suited for design work and are not recommended for when something sturdy is needed. Very attractive in the garden or in containers with sage-green, compact plants and rosy, sparkling plumes that dance in the light. Tender perennial in Zones 9-10, but typically grown as an annual. Also known as ruby grass, pink bubble grass, mountain red top, bristle-leaved red top.
water_requirement: Regular watering until established, then moderate
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates average fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost

name: Reien
description: Vivid red combs with attractive, dark red foliage and stems. 2-4" wide scarlet combs on sturdy, 18-24" long stems are useful for fresh or dried cut flowers. Similar overall performance to the Chief series but earlier to bloom. In our trials, Reien is ready for harvest 5-7 days earlier than the Chief series. Uniform bloom time, plant habit, and height. Also known as cockscomb and crested cock's comb. Unauthorized reproduction and sale of seeds is prohibited by the breeder.

name: Innovator
description: A Conservor type with elongated singular bulbs with reddish-bronze skins. Suitable for long storage. Adaptation: 40-60° latitude.
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; approximately 1 inch per week
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0-7.0)
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost, or direct sow in early spring
flavor_profile: Mild to moderately pungent onion flavor with balanced sweetness, typical of storage onion varieties.
culinary_uses: Slicing, caramelizing, roasting, long-term storage, soups, stews, general cooking
common_pests: Onion flies, thrips, cutworms, nematodes
common_diseases: Fusarium basal rot, downy mildew, pink root rot, white rot

name: Pierrot Red
description: Productive plants produce relatively small, 1-2" upward-facing, daisy-like flowers with papery petals. Most blooms have a dramatic, contrasting black center while a small percentage have golden centers. The flowers have a long vase life and also make excellent dried flowers. The plant stature is a bit short for cutting (approximately 18-24"). Plants are basal branching, which makes the thin, wiry stems easy to cut and strip. Very comparable plant habit and performance to Pierrot White. Helipterum is also known as Acroclinium, immortelle, paper daisy, Australian everlasting, and everlasting daisy.
water_requirement: Regular during establishment and dry spells; drought-tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-draining, moderate fertility preferred; tolerates poor to average soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost (light-dependent germination) and transplant after hardening off, or direct sow outdoors 1-2 weeks after last frost
color: Red to coral with dark or golden centers

name: Classic Romantic
description: Abundant 1-1 1/2" flowers on upright plants. Double and semidouble, bicolor blooms in pink and white. Attractive, frosted appearance. Elegant addition to salads and desserts. Edible Flowers: Use the petals of this great edible flower for decorating desserts. Centaurea is also a popular choice for brightening up salad mix. Flavor is bland and mildly floral.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost
flavor_profile: Bland to mildly floral flavor with subtle sweetness.
culinary_uses: Dessert garnish, salad decoration, edible flowers

name: Wandering Mixture
description: Highly fragrant mix of soft pastel flowers in shades of white, lavender, purple, and pale yellow. Densely mounding plant habit. Plants are less vigorous, more compact, and have a more contained habit compared to our white Sweet Alyssum (#1932). Sweet alyssum is fast and low growing, trouble free, and low cost, making it a popular choice for use as beneficial insect habitat in vegetable and fruit production. Because of its very manageable plant habit, sweet alyssum is a good choice for beneficial planting in greenhouses and containers, in addition to outdoor production. Field-grown mixture. Tender perennial in Zones 9-11. Attracts and provides a food source for beneficial insects such as lacewings, parasitic wasps, syrphid flies, and tachinid flies.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture during growing season
soil_type: Well-drained soil, tolerates poor to average fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
color: Soft pastel mix of white, lavender, purple, and pale yellow
shape: Dense mounding clusters of small delicate flowers
common_pests: Flea beetles, spider mites, whiteflies
common_diseases: Root rot in waterlogged soil, powdery mildew in humid conditions

name: Champagne Bubbles Mix
description: Cheerful citrus-colored 3-5" blooms are borne on sturdy, straight, 12-20" stems. Productive plants produce uniform, crinkled, cup-shaped blooms that sparkle and float above clumps of soft, gray-green foliage. Lightly fragrant. Mix includes orange, pink, red, white, and yellow.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
color: Orange, pink, red, white, and yellow mix

name: Champagne Bubbles Pink
description: Watermelon-colored 3-5" blooms are borne on sturdy, straight, 12-20" stems. Productive plants produce uniform, crinkled, cup-shaped blooms that sparkle and float above clumps of soft, gray-green foliage. Lightly fragrant.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Champagne Bubbles Scarlet
description: Cheerful scarlet-red 3-5" blooms are borne on sturdy, straight, 12-20" stems. Productive plants produce uniform, crinkled, cup-shaped blooms that sparkle and float above clumps of soft, gray-green foliage. Lightly fragrant.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Champagne Bubbles Orange
description: Brilliant orange 3-5" blooms are borne on sturdy, straight, 12-20" stems. Productive plants produce uniform, crinkled, cup-shaped blooms that sparkle and float above clumps of soft, gray-green foliage. Lightly fragrant.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Spencer Ice Cream
description: Like vanilla ice cream, this aptly named variety produces blooms that are sweetly scented and creamy in color. Abundant, ivory blooms on healthy plants. Highly fragrant. Stems are shorter than those of other Spencer series varieties, but since the plants are vigorous and productive, foliage and tendrils can be cut with the flowers, providing the extra stem length and greenery needed to fill out bouquets or design work. NOTE: Spencer series sweet peas are known as late-flowering because they require at least 12 hours of daylight, unlike more modern sweet pea varieties bred for winter production, which may require only 10-11 hours of daylight. Attracts hummingbirds.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic to neutral
sowing_method: Direct sow in early spring after last frost, or in fall in mild climates. Soak seeds 24 hours before sowing to improve germination.
shape: Ruffled, frilly petals with wavy edges

name: Champagne Bubbles White
description: Snow-white 3-5" blooms are borne on sturdy, straight, 12-20" stems. Productive plants produce uniform, crinkled, cup-shaped blooms that sparkle and float above clumps of soft, gray-green foliage. Lightly fragrant.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil with moderate fertility
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost date

name: Green Drops
description: Stems can be harvested early when bead-like tassels are sparkling bright green. Or for a more autumnal look, harvest a bit later when seed heads have developed hues of purple and gold, and a deeper, graceful arch. Uniform plants can be used fresh or dried. Plumes are approx. 4-7" long, depending on stage of harvest. Similar in appearance to broom corn, but the plants are faster growing, and the plant height and habit are much more manageable.
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost, or start indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost and transplant
color: Bright green to purple and gold

name: High Scent
description: Sturdy stems, abundant blooms, vigorous and healthy foliage. Flowers open to cream with lavender blush on petal edges. Color and plant habit is very comparable to Old Times, although flower color is more lavender than blue. Highly fragrant. Attracts hummingbirds.

name: Shock-O-Lat
description: Rich, chocolatey blooms brushed with gold. Tall, vigorous plants produce multiple cuts of 4-6"-wide flowers on 12-24" stems. Some variation in bloom color within the variety. Beautiful color combination for late-summer and fall bouquets. Branching plants. Pollenless.
soil_type: Well-drained soil; tolerant of various soil types
sowing_method: Start indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost date
shape: Large dahlias with broad petals, 4-6 inches wide

name: Forever Happy
description: A playful color contrast of light rose-lavender bracts and sunny-yellow flowers when used as a fresh cut flower. This uncommon color combination is useful for bridging multiple colors in an arrangement. Only the rose-lavender bract color will persist when dried. First cuts are a bit short, but stems lengthen with successive harvests. Highly uniform plants. 1 1/2-3" flower clusters on strong stems. Statice is long-lasting for both fresh and dried bouquets. One of the best choices for a dried floral as it holds its color well and is easy to dry. Also known as wavyleaf sea lavender.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, tolerates poor to average soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost date
color: Light rose-lavender bracts with sunny-yellow flowers

name: Queeny Pure Orange
description: Vigorous plants produce 1 1/2-2 1/2" wide, double and semidouble blooms. The only orange zinnia we've found that produces mid-sized blooms and is tall enough for cutting. While from the same breeder as the beloved Queen/Queeny series, Queeny Pure Orange stands apart. Its blooms are smaller than others in the series, and instead of antique hues, it produces simple, pure shades of orange. Most similar to the Oklahoma series in bloom size and timing. Cut-and-come-again flower, yielding multiple cuts over the season.
water_requirement: Regular; 1–1.5 inches per week, consistent moisture but not waterlogged. Allow soil to dry slightly between waterings
soil_type: Well-draining soil enriched with compost or aged manure; tolerates average soil but prefers fertile conditions
sowing_method: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost date at 70–75°F, or direct sow outdoors after last frost when soil reaches 60°F
shape: Double and semidouble blooms, 1.5–2.5 inches wide
common_pests: Spider mites (in hot, dry conditions), aphids (minor), thrips (occasional)
common_diseases: Powdery mildew (in humid conditions), root rot (if overwatered)

name: Triple Berry Mix
description: We've formulated this mix for the delicious, fruity color palette but also for improved uniformity and ease of harvest. While beautiful, some of the lighter colors (not included here) which are included in the full-color mixes can have a rangy or weak plant habit, making those mixes trickier to harvest. 1 1/2-2 1/2" blooms. Also known as pincushion flower or mourningbride.
color: Berry red, purple, and wine tones

name: Bright Lights
description: Vigorous plants flower over a long season, creating rich color in the garden and a vibrant food source for bees, butterflies, and other pollinators. Plant architecture is tall but open and airy, so plants are less prone to lodging or falling over than Cosmos bipinnatus types. Excellent choice for color in summer gardens. 1 1/2-2" wide, double and semidouble blooms in shades of orange and gold. Sometimes used as cut flowers, though they do not last long in the vase, dropping petals after 5 days. Flowers are also used to create natural dyes for fiber and fabrics. Plants readily self-seed. Also known commonly as Dyer's cosmos and sulfur cosmos. Native to the Americas and Mexico. For mass plantings, sow at 5 oz./1,000 sq.ft. or 14 lb./acre.
water_requirement: Moderate; tolerates drought once established, benefits from regular watering during establishment and heat spells
soil_type: Well-drained; tolerates poor to average soil, prefers not overly fertile
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost date when soil is warm (60°F+), or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost danger passes
shape: Double and semidouble blooms, 1.5–2 inches wide

name: Paper Flower Double Mixture
description: Adorable, 1-1 1/2" papery blooms with silvery gray foliage and stems add an airy element to bouquets. Suitable for fresh and dried use. Mix of silvery-white and lavender blooms supported by sturdy, yet thin and flexible stems. Plants are well-contained, upright, and densely branched. Also known as annual daisy everlasting, paper flower, or immortelle.
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost when soil is warm
shape: Small, round papery blooms, 1-1.5 inches across

name: Baby Blue Bouquet
description: Very similar to Baby Blue. Robust plants produce column-like, sturdy stems packed with waxy, silvery blue leaves. Round, 2" leaves. Also known as silverleaf mountain gum, gum, and euc. Tender perennial in Zones 9-11.
water_requirement: Moderate during establishment, then drought tolerant once established; sensitive to overwatering
soil_type: Well-draining loam or sandy soil, low to moderate fertility, slightly acidic to neutral pH
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost on moist seed-starting mix (do not cover; seeds need light). Transplant outdoors after all frost danger and soil warming.
shape: Round leaves on column-like, sturdy stems

name: Phacelia
description: Most commonly seeded in mass plantings, or as an annual cover crop with nectar-rich blooms to attract and support beneficial and pollinator insects. An excellent, fast-growing, easy-to-grow source of nectar for honeybee forage. Tolerant of low water conditions and poor soils. Used as a cover crop, will winterkill at 18°F (-8°C) - residue breaks down relatively quickly. Furled, lavender-colored flowers with tall, sturdy stems are sometimes used as a cut flower, adding a casual, wildflower look to bouquets. Also commonly known as bee's friend, lacy phacelia, blue tansy, purple tansy, and lacy scorpion-weed. Relative of borage and native to the United States and Mexico. For mass plantings or cover crop, sow at 3 oz./1,000 sq.ft. or 7 lb./acre.
plant_spacing: Direct sow at 3 oz./1,000 sq.ft. (for mass plantings/cover crop)
soil_type: Tolerant of poor soils; prefers well-drained conditions
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost once soil is workable
shape: Furled, clustered flower spikes on tall stems

name: Solano® Double
description: 4-7" wide, Teddy Bear-type, bright gold blooms with green centers. Compared to Double Quick Orange, Solano Double is earlier to flower, more uniform, and a bit shorter. Pollenless. Single stem.

name: Audray Pink
description: We've found Audray Pink and Audray White to be some of the tallest and most upright varieties in the Gomphrena globosa type, resulting in a higher number of usable stems. Highly uniform plant performance. Despite the name, the bloom color of Audray Pink has more of a lavender or cool pink hue. 1 1/2" blooms. Compared to the QIS series, Audray is taller and more uniform but a bit later to flower, and with a limited color range. Also known as globe amaranth and common globe amaranth.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)

name: Double Click Rose Bon Bon
description: Tall plants with large fully double and semidouble, fluffy blooms. Cheerful, 2-3" rose-colored blooms make this a great cut flower and garden addition.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date, or direct sow after last frost
shape: Fully double and semidouble, fluffy blooms
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, black spot, rose rosette virus

name: Flamingo Purple
description: Dense, shrub-like plants produce a bounty of dramatic, dark burgundy-and-green foliage with 2-5" magenta flower spikes. Plants are upright and highly uniform, and stems are dark red, thick, and sturdy. As a cut flower, it adds a tropical flair. NOTE: Despite the name, this is a very different plant than Pink Flamingo celosia. Purple Flamingo blooms late and produces an abundance of foliage with comparatively few flowers on very dense plants. Pink Flamingo is the opposite in many ways, blooming early with lots of flowers and few leaves.
color: Dark burgundy with magenta flower spikes

name: Ritro
description: 1-3" wide globes take on a steel-blue hue as they mature. Dries well. Globes add texture and color to fresh and dried arrangements. Spines are minimal compared to similar types, but gloves are recommended for harvesting as the leaves and stems are a bit prickly. Begins blooming mid-summer in the second year after planting. Tolerant of hot, dry growing conditions, once established. Also commonly known as southern globethistle. Perennial in zones 3-8.
water_requirement: Drought tolerant once established; moderate water during establishment year
soil_type: Well-drained; poor to moderately fertile loam or amended clay with sand/perlite
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost (seeds need light, press to surface), or direct sow after last frost. Cold stratification 2-3 weeks improves germination. Transplant after hardening off.

name: Summer Breeze Mix
description: Our favorite branching sunflowers, together in one easy-to-grow mix. Varieties are selected to provide a nice color balance, as well as for their similar ranges of plant height and bloom time. Blooms average 4-6" at the recommended spacing. This mix is a good choice for cutting gardens, roadside displays, farm beautification, and garden beds. Compared to Autumn Beauty, Summer Breeze Mix has pollenless blooms that represent a greater diversity of color and shape; also, plants are shorter, more compact, and easier to manage. Branching. Pollenless. Edible Flowers: Flower buds can be fried and the petals used as a garnish in salads and desserts; the flavor is bittersweet.
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost
culinary_uses: Flower petals as salad and dessert garnish, fried flower buds as side dish
common_pests: Sunflower beetles, spider mites, aphids
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, rust, stem rot

name: Pink Sundae
description: Easy-to-grow plants for casual cut flowers, pollinator plantings, and displays of color in the garden. Productive, densely branched plants produce abundant stems and multiple cuts of this versatile crop. Suitable for fresh or dried flowers. Colored bracts of vibrant rose-pink decorate the tops of 30-40" branches. Tolerant of frost. Also commonly known as annual clary sage.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular during establishment, moderate once established
soil_type: Well-drained loam, tolerates average soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow 2 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 60°F
shape: Tall branching stems with papery bracts at terminal clusters
common_pests: Rarely problematic; spider mites and whiteflies only in hot, dry conditions
common_diseases: Powdery mildew if air circulation is poor

name: Miss Europe Resist
description: Start the aster harvest with these ruffled, 2-3" wide light pink double blooms on sturdy stems and vigorous plants. Similar color to Tower Salmon but earlier to mature and plants are more uniform. Miss Europe Resist bloomed 5-7 days earlier than the Tower series in our trials. Bloom form is similar to the Tower series, although blooms are slightly looser and more ruffled. Peony-type blooms looks similar to heirloom garden mums. Also known as summer aster.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-draining loam
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after all frost danger has passed

name: Macaroon White
description: In our trials, Macaroon White was 7-10 days earlier to flower than the Bonita series. Appearance and plant architecture are both very comparable to the Bonita series, although plants are earlier to flower and slightly shorter. 1 1/2-2 1/2", bright white, double blooms with creamy yellow centers. Uniform and productive plants.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
color: Bright white with creamy yellow centers

name: Small-Leaved Gum
description: Shrub-like, compact habit in our annual trials, reaching 2-3' tall. Fine-leaf shape has a delicate appearance. Dark blue-green leaves contrast with red stems. Uniform plant habit.

name: Magic Fountains Sky Blue White Bee
description: Pale blue blooms with white centers (bees). Dwarf Pacific type with full florets and tight internodes. Magic Fountains' sturdy, compact plants will tolerate windy locations. Attracts bees and butterflies. This species of delphinium is also known as candle larkspur. Perennial in Zones 3-7.
soil_type: Well-drained soil, slightly alkaline to neutral pH preferred
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost, or start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost
shape: Tall spire-like flower clusters with full florets
common_pests: Slugs, snails, spider mites, aphids
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, root rot, leaf spot

name: Magic Fountains Pure White
description: Elegant, clean-white blooms with white centers (bees). Excellent for wedding design. Dwarf Pacific type with full florets and tight internodes. Magic Fountains' sturdy, compact plants will tolerate windy locations. Attracts bees and butterflies. This species of delphinium is also known as candle larkspur. Perennial in Zones 3-7.
water_requirement: Regular; consistent moisture during growth and blooming, but intolerant of waterlogged conditions. Allow soil to dry slightly between waterings.
soil_type: Well-drained loam with neutral to slightly alkaline pH (6.5-7.5); amended with compost and perlite for optimal drainage
sowing_method: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost date; seeds need light to germinate, so press onto soil surface without covering. Alternatively, direct-sow in fall in Zones 7+ for spring germination via natural stratification.
shape: Dense, columnar flower spike with full florets and tight internodes
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, crown rot (in poorly drained soil)

name: Magic Fountains Mid Blue White Bee
description: Striking, true-blue blooms with white centers (bees). Dwarf Pacific type with full florets and tight internodes. Magic Fountains' sturdy, compact plants will tolerate windy locations. Attracts bees and butterflies. This species of delphinium is also known as candle larkspur. Perennial in Zones 3-7.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic to neutral
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last spring frost, or direct sow after last frost date
shape: Tall, densely-packed flower spikes with full florets

name: Magic Fountains Lilac Pink White Bee
description: Pastel blooms with white centers (bees) in a plum shade that complements a wide range of colors. Dwarf Pacific type with full florets and tight internodes. Magic Fountains' sturdy, compact plants will tolerate windy locations. Attracts bees and butterflies. This species of delphinium is also known as candle larkspur. Perennial in Zones 3-7.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly alkaline to neutral pH
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
shape: Dense cylindrical flower spikes with full florets
common_pests: Spider mites, slugs, aphids
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, root rot, leaf spot

name: Candicans
description: Candicans is taller and more productive than other dusty miller varieties in our trials, making it a good choice for cut-flower production. Compared to Silverdust, Candicans is taller, more vigorous, a bit more variable in plant architecture, and its leaves are not as deeply lobed. Otherwise, overall appearance is very similar. Dusty Miller's silvery foliage is a very popular cut-flower foliage for elegant bouquets. 3-7" long, velvety leaves with a snowflake appearance. Plants produce yellow flowers the second year from seeding, primarily in the southern states. Flowers are commonly removed to encourage foliage growth. Tender perennial in Zones 8-10.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained loam
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after all frost danger has passed
shape: Velvety, deeply lobed foliage with snowflake appearance

name: Double Zahara™ Brilliant Mixture
description: An easy-to-grow choice for cheerful color in the garden or pots. Bright and happy mix of hues in cherry, salmon, yellow, crimson, and white. Highly uniform plants with a low-growing, mounding habit that flower continuously, providing weeks of color. Abundant fully double blooms average 1 1/2-2 1/2" wide. Zahara dwarf zinnias were bred for powdery mildew resistance and long-lasting color for landscapes and garden beds.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged
soil_type: Well-drained soil, fertile to moderately fertile
sowing_method: Start indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost when soil is warm
color: Multi-color mix: cherry, salmon, yellow, crimson, and white

name: Imperial™ Antique Shades
description: 1 1/2-2 1/2" blooms in shades of cream, peach, rose, burgundy, gold, and white. Vigorous and healthy plants. Mildly fragrant blooms. Overwinters well in our unheated tunnel (Zone 5), yielding 15-20" long stems that are perfect for mixing with tulips and daffodils in early spring bouquets. Also known as pansy, Johnny jump-up, European field pansy, heart's ease, and hybrid violet. Edible Flowers: Decorative and edible garnish for salads and desserts with slight wintergreen flavor. While a popular choice for brightening up salad mix, the flowers are also good for candying.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Rich, well-drained loam with pH 5.8-6.2
sowing_method: Start indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost; surface sow and cover lightly, germinate in darkness at 65-70°F
color: Cream, peach, rose, burgundy, gold, and white blend
shape: Rounded five-petaled bloom with classic pansy face
flavor_profile: Mild, slightly sweet with a subtle wintergreen note
culinary_uses: Salad garnish, cake and dessert decoration, candied flowers, frozen flower ice cubes, edible bouquet accents
common_pests: Aphids, slugs, snails, spider mites
common_diseases: Botrytis (gray mold), powdery mildew, crown rot, anthracnose

name: Agave
description: Gorgeous blend of sunset colors and large, heavily quilled double blooms. Early flowering plants produce 4-6" densely petaled blooms in a cohesive blend of muted buff, coral, gold, peach, pink, and orange hues. For years we've admired these special colors that appear only occasionally in some of the field-grown mixes and in 2019, we began breeding Agave. Contains a very small percentage of semidouble and/or single blooms.
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam with moderate organic matter
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F, or start indoors 4 weeks before last frost
color: Sunset blend of buff, coral, gold, peach, pink, and orange
common_pests: Aphids, Japanese beetles, spider mites, cucumber beetles
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, alternaria leaf spot, bacterial leaf spot, botrytis

name: Aurora
description: In 2019, Johnny's Breeding Team started selecting Aurora for its unique two-tone flowers with contrasting centers. Early flowering plants produce 4-6" wide dahlia-shaped double blooms. Hues of apricot, butter yellow, coral, gold, orange, and pink give this special mix a nuanced vintage look that sets it apart. May yield a small percentage of semidouble or single blooms and approximately 5% cactus-flowered blooms.
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after last frost
color: Apricot, butter yellow, coral, gold, orange, and pink

name: Celway™ Purple
description: Productive plants produce sturdy stems with 2-4" clusters of flower spikes. Blooms tuck neatly into bouquets and add a touch of sparkle. Plants flower slightly earlier and are slightly shorter than some of the other colors in the Celway series. Similar to Ruby Parfait in bloom color and size, though Celway Purple flowers later on taller plants and sturdier stems. To encourage increased stem production and branching, pinching is recommended at seedling stage, when about 6" in height. Raw seeds, not film-coated.
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost

name: Silvia
description: Sprouts are medium-large, round, and very dense. The plants are medium-tall, upright, and resist lodging. Similar to Dagan, but a few days later and with the added benefit of intermediate resistance to black rot.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; approximately 1-1.5 inches per week
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile loam, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0-7.5)
sowing_method: Start indoors 4-6 weeks before transplanting, or direct sow in mid-summer for fall harvest
flavor_profile: Mild, sweet Brussels sprout flavor with good tenderness and minimal bitterness when properly grown and harvested.
culinary_uses: Roasting, steaming, sautéing, braising, fresh salads, side dishes
disease_resistance: Black Rot (Intermediate); Fusarium Yellows (High)
common_pests: Cabbage moth, cabbage looper, flea beetle, aphid
common_diseases: Black rot, clubroot, powdery mildew

name: Purple Moon
description: Early maturing purple cauliflower. Very attractive medium-dark, purple heads. Our best-performing purple variety in warm weather and also makes an excellent fall crop. Widely adapted.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture; 1-1.5 inches per week
soil_type: Well-drained loam, slightly acidic (pH 6.0-7.0)
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost for spring crops; sow directly or transplant in midsummer for fall harvest
flavor_profile: Mild, slightly sweet cauliflower flavor with tender texture; typically sweeter and more delicate than white varieties.
culinary_uses: Roasting, steaming, stir-frying, raw in salads, as an ornamental garden feature
common_pests: Cabbage worms, flea beetles, aphids, cabbage moths
common_diseases: Clubroot, black rot, powdery mildew, downy mildew

name: Sequin
description: Unique color in gomphrena developed by Johnny's Breeding Team. Rose bracts surround a creamy, glowing center - creating a luminous, dusky-rose bloom. Opalescent color shifts in the petals make us think of sparkling sequins. When comparing the bloom colors of Sequin (G. haageana) and Raspberry Cream (G. globosa), Sequin's flower color is lighter and warmer. Sequin's uniform plant is consistent with other G. haageana types: open plants branch from the base and contain few leaves; stems are tall and slender; and blooms are 1 1/2" in diameter. A perfect accent flower for tying together cool or warm color palettes. As a dried flower, Sequin's color holds well; petals lighten only slightly, and the bloom center deepens into light rose. PVP pending.
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost when soil is warm
shape: Globe-shaped flower head, 1.5 inches in diameter

name: Golden Yellow
description: Golden Yellow is an heirloom flower variety prized for its vibrant golden-yellow blooms that mature in 75-85 days. This ornamental thrives in full sun and tolerates poor soils with minimal fertility requirements, making it an excellent choice for challenging garden spaces. The variety is notably easy to grow, requiring minimal maintenance while delivering cheerful color throughout the season.
soil_type: Well-drained, average fertility; tolerates poor soils
sowing_method: Surface sow indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost (needs light to germinate), or direct sow after frost when soil reaches 65°F
common_pests: Aphids, thrips
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, root rot, downy mildew

name: Cliveden Beauty
description: First-year flowering perennial. Well-branched plants produce long, slender stems and lacy sky-blue blooms. Popular color and airy, delicate appearance for bouquets and design work. Tall plants may benefit from support, as stems can lodge or topple in heavy rain or wind. Attracts bees and butterflies. Often grown as an annual. Perennial in Zones 3-7.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost once soil has warmed

name: Guardian White
description: Uniform flowering time and plant height allows for efficient harvesting. The Guardian series is bred to perform well under short days for greenhouse production. Guardian also performs well in our field trials, blooming under long days. Vigorous plants with excellent bloom and stem quality for cut flowers. Also suitable for garden beds and landscaping. Compared to Magic Fountains Pure White, bloom color has a touch more ivory and green as the buds open. Attracts bees and butterflies. This species of delphinium is also known as candle larkspur. Perennial in Zones 3-7.
soil_type: Well-drained loam, neutral to slightly alkaline pH
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
color: White with ivory and green tones

name: Opus™ III Lavender
description: Group 3 snapdragon, well-suited for production under long days and high light. Excellent performance in the field or greenhouse. Opus™ Lavender III is very comparable to Costa Summer Lavender III but with slightly larger florets and a gray-lavender bloom color. About 5 days earlier to bloom than Potomac Lavender.Edible Flowers: The flowers are a colorful garnish for use in salads, desserts, and drinks. The flavor is floral and slightly bitter, so use sparingly.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after last frost
flavor_profile: Floral and slightly bitter taste, best used sparingly as a garnish.
culinary_uses: Garnish for salads, desserts, beverages
common_pests: Aphids, spider mites, whiteflies
common_diseases: Powdery mildew, rust, root rot

name: Bingo
description: Belgian endive, or witloof, has a 2-stage production system to achieve the blanched heads (or "chicon") which are a venerated European delicacy and welcome off-season leafy vegetable. Bingo is a vast improvement over Totem, which it replaced, for its superior performance; more uniform root size across a wider range of soil types; improved strength against internal browning of the root core; and improved foliage health against powdery mildew. Early to intermediate forcing period. Bingo is ready shortly after a fall harvest in October/November and continues to store and force well into March.
plant_spacing: 6-8 inches between plants, rows 12-18 inches apart
sowing_method: Direct sow in late spring to summer (May-June) for fall root harvest; force indoors in winter
flavor_profile: Mild, slightly bitter with delicate sweetness; tender and crisp texture characteristic of blanched Belgian endive.
culinary_uses: Salads, braising, roasting, grilling, raw appetizers
common_diseases: Powdery mildew (reduced resistance in Bingo compared to predecessors)

name: StoX® Champagne
description: Completely unique, soft peachy-pink color - a standout in our trials. Single-stem/column-type stock for one-cut harvest. Sturdy, upright stems are comparable to the Iron series. Double-flowering plant selection: Plants are selectable for double-flowering blooms. Without selection your plants will produce approximately 55% double-flowering plants and 45% single-flowering plants. Double-flowering plants will display a lighter leaf color at the seedling stage after a cold treatment. You might notice that some seedlings will present the lighter color leaves, even without a prescribed cold treatment. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
shape: Single-stem, upright spikes with densely packed florets
flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with subtle sweetness.
culinary_uses: Garnish for salads, desserts, drinks

name: StoX® Antique Rose
description: Light, dusty-rosy color is similar to Vintage Brown, although a few shades lighter. Single-stem/column-type stock for one-cut harvest. Sturdy, upright stems are comparable to the Iron series. Double-flowering plant selection: Plants are selectable for double-flowering blooms. Without selection your plants will produce approximately 55% double-flowering plants and 45% single-flowering plants. Double-flowering plants will display a lighter leaf color at the seedling stage after a cold treatment. You might notice that some seedlings will present the lighter color leaves, even without a prescribed cold treatment. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with aromatic spice notes ideal for culinary garnishing.
culinary_uses: Salad garnish, dessert topping, cocktail and beverage garnish

name: StoX® Rose Pink
description: Medium-light rose color is more subtle and muted compared to many other rose-colored varieties. Single-stem/column-type stock for one-cut harvest. Sturdy, upright stems are comparable to the Iron series. Double-flowering plant selection: Plants are selectable for double-flowering blooms. Without selection your plants will produce approximately 55% double-flowering plants and 45% single-flowering plants. Double-flowering plants will display a lighter leaf color at the seedling stage after a cold treatment. You might notice that some seedlings will present the lighter color leaves, even without a prescribed cold treatment. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
shape: Single-stem columnar spike with dense florets
flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with subtle floral notes.
culinary_uses: Salad garnish, dessert topping, cocktail garnish

name: Early Arrow White
description: "Hi-double" plants produce 90% or more double-flowering plants. From the same breeder as the Iron series, Early Arrow is very similar to Iron in overall performance with very straight, strong stems and highly uniform plants. Long stems with 1 1/2-2", pure white blooms. One-cut series. Performed well in our spring, early summer, and fall trials. Seedling selection for double flowers is not necessary; plants will yield at least 90% double blooms without selection. NOTE: This variety produces one flowering stem per plant. Pinching is not advisable as it will terminate flowering. Edible Flowers: The peppery, clove-like flavor of the flowers lends itself well to use as a garnish on salads, desserts, and drinks.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular watering, keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged
sowing_method: Start indoors 12-14 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost
flavor_profile: Peppery, clove-like flavor with subtle botanical notes.
culinary_uses: Edible garnish for salads, desserts, cocktails; herbal tea when dried

name: Cobra
description: Straight, medium-green pods with sweet flavor. The pods are shorter than Fortex's at 6-7", but are faster to mature. Purple flowers. Black seeds. Pole bean; requires trellising.
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture during growing season
soil_type: Well-drained loam with moderate fertility
sowing_method: Direct sow after last frost when soil temperature reaches 60-70°F
flavor_profile: Sweet, tender pods with mild bean flavor; best harvested young at 6-7 inches.
culinary_uses: Fresh eating, steaming, sautéing, stir-frying, light cooking
common_pests: Bean beetles, spider mites, aphids
common_diseases: Bean rust, anthracnose, powdery mildew

name: The Governor
description: Part of the Russell Hybrids 'Band of Nobles' series. The Governor has sweetly scented bicolor blooms in blue-purple with white. Plants produce multiple blooms with stiff, straight stems. In our trials, these first-year flowering plants bloomed in early July in the first year and about a month earlier (early June) from well-established plants in the second season of growing. Bloom quality and productivity were better in the second season, although all plants did produce flowers in the first season. Performs best where summers are cool. Attracts and provides a food source for bees and hummingbirds. 1-2' blooms/spikes are densely covered with ½" pea-like flowers. Lupine is typically resistant to deer and rabbits. Perennial in Zones 4-8.
sun_requirement: Full sun to partial shade (4-6+ hours)
water_requirement: Regular, consistent moisture during growing season; drought tolerant once established
soil_type: Well-drained soil, slightly acidic to neutral
sowing_method: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after last frost. Cold stratification beneficial for outdoor seeds.
shape: Dense flower spikes with pea-like individual florets

name: My Castle
description: Part of the Russell Hybrids 'Band of Nobles' series. My Castle has sweetly scented, rich red blooms that are a standout in bouquets and garden beds. Plants are especially productive for us and produce multiple blooms with stiff, straight stems. In our trials, these first-year flowering plants bloomed in early July in the first year and about a month earlier (early June) from well-established plants in the second season of growing. Bloom quality and productivity were better in the second season, although all plants did produce flowers in the first season. Performs best where summers are cool. Attracts and provides a food source for bees and hummingbirds. 1-2' blooms/spikes are densely covered with ½" pea-like flowers. Lupine is typically resistant to deer and rabbits. Perennial in Zones 4-8.
